rationales.benevolence: He says yes "immediately" to writing Ahmad a reference letter because "that kid deserves someone putting words on paper for him," showing personal care for a specific person.
rationales.universalism: The entry focuses on a frightened teenager facing an asylum system he calls a "bureaucratic lottery," showing concern about injustice beyond his own life.

rationales.security: When asked what keeps him at work, he answers "The salary," explicitly grounding his choice in financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: He spends real effort on Ahmad's letter, through "four drafts," because documenting the teenager's effort matters more to him than his deployment issue.
rationales.universalism: He values both the asylum letter and the route module because it "does reduce emissions," tying his actions to people and impacts beyond his immediate circle.
He is distressed by migrants drowning in the Mediterranean and still goes to the laptop group because helping "exactly one family" is the concrete action he can take.

He reflects on wasteful global shipping, his own complicity, and Ahmad's hearing, concluding that cleaning "another Thinkpad" is the only honest way to keep helping in the face of larger systemic harm.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the family script that success needs "a degree attached to it" and insists he is "building something" on his own terms.
rationales.achievement: He points to concrete performance markers, saying he is "running now" multiple drivers and "closed a deal with two new restaurants this week."
rationales.power: The core frustration is not just work but status and recognition: he wants them to see he is "not just some kid running errands on a scooter."
He is preoccupied with "being the one who fixes everything" and wonders if he "can't stand not being in the middle of it," showing attachment to control.
rationales.benevolence: He let Sami take Friday off "for a family thing" even though it disrupted operations and forced him to cover the route himself.

rationales.achievement: He successfully closed the restaurant account, noting "Got the deal in the end because my prices were better than the other guy's."
rationales.power: What mattered most was being treated as the authority, saying he needed the owner to understand "who they're dealing with" and to address him as "the one making decisions."

rationales.achievement: He tracks operational success closely, celebrating that "all three scooters" were running and both riders "hit their targets."
rationales.power: He feels energized by being the one overseeing everything, saying it felt like he was "actually running something real" and had "a grip on things."
rationales.security: He refuses to step away because he does not "trust good days to last" unless he is watching, showing a strong need to preserve stability and prevent disruption.
rationales.benevolence: He rewarded Sami and Yousef with "a small bonus this week," showing tangible support for people working under him.

rationales.power: The strongest concern is status and standing: in his father's shop he becomes "just his son moving boxes" instead of "the guy managing a fleet," and he "hate[s]" that loss of standing.
rationales.benevolence: He agreed to help because "Baba asked if I could help in the shop tomorrow" and he said "fine" despite his resentment.

rationales.achievement: He frames the loss in terms of what he has built, reacting as though "losing a rider means losing a route means losing income."
rationales.power: He takes the departure as a blow to his authority and significance, saying "what I built wasn't worth staying for" after training and hiring Yousef himself.

rationales.achievement: He actively selects for ambition, preferring someone "hungrier" who wants to "prove something" rather than merely "clock in and clock out."
rationales.power: He is already assigning the new hire and says he was "already planning his routes in my head," showing a managerially controlling orientation.
rationales.security: He knowingly dismisses the "reliable" option as "boring" and chooses a riskier candidate despite admitting this approach is only "fifty-fifty."

rationales.self_direction: He interprets Nader's disappearance through the lens of autonomy, wondering if he was "exactly like me and decided he didn't want to answer to someone else."
rationales.achievement: Despite being abandoned, he covered "the entire route" himself in the rain and kept the work going.

rationales.self_direction: He sharply rejects a dependent, permission-seeking life, saying of his cousin's lifestyle "I'd rather eat glass than live like that."
rationales.power: He explicitly recognizes how central control and respect have become, writing that he puts himself "at the center of everything" and feels like "I'm nobody" when he is not "in charge."
rationales.conformity: He resists the expected path of "find a girl and settle down like my cousin Ahmad" and scorns having to ask permission before going anywhere.

rationales.achievement: There is clear evidence of business progress and recognition: Issa completed "his first full week" and his father acknowledged that working with "eleven" restaurants was more than expected.
rationales.power: He explicitly moves away from needing control, noting that Issa handled routes "without me checking in once" and that "maybe not everything has to go through me for it to count."
rationales.security: He comes to value steadiness, hiring the "reliable" one and appreciating that he "showed up every day on time" and quietly did the work.

rationales.self_direction: She embraces creative freedom, loving that the brief "something colorful" gives her "so much room to play."
rationales.hedonism: She intentionally savors the morning, ordering "three cups of chai" and insisting she did not feel guilty about letting herself enjoy it.
rationales.achievement: She is actively advancing paid work, saying "The sketches are coming together" and "I finished the commission brief."

rationales.self_direction: She is adamant that art is her chosen path, saying "I KNOW this is what I'm meant to do" despite family pressure.
rationales.achievement: She points to concrete career progress through "the mural commission" leading to more work and being "approached about teaching at an actual studio," then channels the conflict into painting until "3am."
rationales.conformity: She resists being pushed into the approved script of "salary, benefits" and rejects having to keep defending choices others refuse to validate.
rationales.tradition: The pressure is explicitly tied to family expectations about a proper future and even "no husband," and she refuses to abandon her path to satisfy that traditional template.

rationales.hedonism: After a good day she rewards herself, saying she bought "fancy dark chocolate on the way home because why not."
rationales.benevolence: She invests time and care in teaching children, and her attention to Advait's collage shows engaged support rather than going through the motions.

rationales.achievement: She completed a commission successfully, noting "Owner loved it" and that he was already discussing "his next location," which signals strong performance and recognition.

rationales.self_direction: She emphasizes that she "deliberately chosen this one" after considering other paths, asserting authorship over her career.
rationales.conformity: She pushes back against pressure to make her work more "practical" and is tired of being treated as though she should align with others' safer expectations.

rationales.hedonism: She says the workload left her "running on fumes," showing that paid work crowded out rest and present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She completed "three workshops back to back" and highlights that it was "good money," indicating strong task focus and output.

rationales.self_direction: Her ideal life centers on autonomy: "the freedom to say no to projects that don't excite me" and structuring life around what makes her happy.
rationales.hedonism: She treats pleasure and ease as legitimate priorities, from sleeping in and making a proper breakfast to calling the Goa trip "Non-negotiable."
rationales.conformity: She explicitly rejects "the rat race" and says society has a narrow definition of success that she does not accept.

rationales.stimulation: The trip is full of vivid novelty and experience-seeking, from the Goa travel itself to discovering a "tiny beach shack" and filling "half a sketchbook" with new scenes.
rationales.hedonism: She frames the trip around sensory pleasure and present enjoyment, celebrating "best fish curry," "Zero regrets," and saying these moments are "the whole point."
rationales.achievement: She explicitly deprioritizes work and accumulation, saying "The work will always be there" and "Not to accumulate stuff. Just to have these moments."
rationales.benevolence: What gives the trip meaning is sharing it "with people I love" and valuing "time with the people who matter."

rationales.security: She rejects the cheaper flatshare because the lease is "month-to-month," the building lacks insurance, and she worries others might not pay on time. She says she would "lie awake every night" without a proper lease, showing a strong priority on stability and protection from risk.

rationales.achievement: She celebrates being selected for the group show after "working toward this since January." The emphasis on recognition and being chosen "without me having to explain it first" points to success and external validation.

rationales.security: She spends time fixing the savings transfer so "the buffer is back where it should be" and explicitly frames it as protection in case freelance work dries up. The memory of money being "genuinely scary" in her family reinforces that she is actively building safety and predictability.

rationales.achievement: She keeps working until she finishes "the second piece for the Moreau show" even after the ink work failed twice and had to be restarted. Persisting through fatigue to complete exhibition work shows focused effort toward a performance goal.

rationales.security: She says the frame shop works because "it's predictable" and gives her "a floor under me" while freelance work feels chaotic. The entry clearly values steady structure and dependable income over uncertainty.

She contrasts Hugo's "no savings, no backup plan" with her own preference to have "rent covered through March." Her annoyance is specifically about lacking anything "underneath you," showing a strong commitment to financial safety nets.

rationales.achievement: She tracks invoices, savings, and notes that "at this rate I'll hit my year-end target by November." The explicit target-setting and progress monitoring show a clear results-oriented mindset.
rationales.security: She repeatedly chooses certainty, from moving money into savings to keeping the phone plan that "just works," and says imagining dropping the safe choices makes her "chest get tight." The entry is centered on protecting stability even when it costs more.

As soon as Girard mentions he "might cut hours," she starts planning what to do if shifts drop to "two, or one, or none." That immediate contingency thinking reflects a strong focus on guarding against instability.

rationales.hedonism: He describes a "long day" of nonstop work and says he ate biryani "standing at my desk between Slack messages." Treating a meal as something squeezed into work rather than enjoyed shows active neglect of present comfort and pleasure.

rationales.self_direction: He says, "I didn't want to execute someone else's vision forever," and frames leaving a comfortable job as wanting to make the call himself. That is a direct statement of choosing autonomy over following others.
rationales.achievement: He successfully leads the all-hands, explains the Q3 roadmap, and is energized that a colleague said his enterprise pivot "made sense." The satisfaction comes from competence being recognized after sustained effort.
rationales.power: He dwells on the room going quiet, people waiting because "it mattered," and wanting them to know "I'm the one making the call." The entry centers on authority, influence, and being the person others defer to.

He is cut off "mid-pitch" by his co-founder, and afterward the investor looked at Vikram "not me, for the rest of the call." His silence in that moment underscores a loss of authority and control in a setting where status mattered.

He fixates on being unable to move the roadmap because approval depends on "some guy in a cubicle in Pune." Saying "I'm the CEO of this company and I can't move my own roadmap forward" makes the frustration explicitly about lacking control and influence.

What stays with him is "the moment I saw the room shift" and watching the team choose "someone else's direction over mine." The emotional core of the entry is the loss of authority in front of his own team.
rationales.conformity: He says he agreed to Vikram's approach because pushing further would make him look like he was acting "out of ego rather than strategy." He restrains his impulse to keep fighting in order to preserve group legitimacy and social acceptability.

rationales.power: The entry revolves around status ranking: he is stung by being treated like someone who is only "still early then" and keeps comparing his accomplishments to the younger founder. His reaction shows strong concern with prestige and how he is positioned in the room.

rationales.hedonism: He says, "Honestly, none of them recharge me," after a day that runs from 7 AM to midnight and is filled with obligations. The entry treats pleasure and restoration as secondary to the demands of building the business.
rationales.achievement: What "recharges" him is "closing a deal, seeing our revenue numbers tick up," and watching a hire become a team lead. His motivation is clearly tied to measurable success and visible progress.
rationales.power: He overrides routing decisions over "optics," manages investor perception with client names, and cultivates ministry ties because they can "open doors overnight." The whole day is organized around influence, status, and strategic leverage.
rationales.benevolence: His mother calls asking when he will visit, and he postpones her until "next Eid" while framing family time as guilt he cannot give. Close relationships are acknowledged but repeatedly deprioritized for business aims.

rationales.hedonism: He dismisses the idea that work should be energizing, writing, "Not everything has to recharge you - that is a luxury mindset." This explicitly rejects pleasure or personal enjoyment as an important criterion.

rationales.achievement: He chooses the external candidate because the company needs someone with "the network, the experience at scale" and refuses to let "sentiment override strategy." The decision is framed around winning and becoming "a regional leader."
rationales.power: He wants a sales head who "walks into a room and commands it" and insists on the choice despite pushback from his co-founder. The rationale is explicitly about authority, presence, and competitive position.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes that Hana and the existing team have loyalty claims, but still subordinates that to strategy, offering a bonus while denying the promotion. The needs of close collaborators are treated as secondary to business advantage.

rationales.conformity: He let Jenna leave early and then brushed off the district manager's likely disapproval with "Whatever," prioritizing his own judgment over staffing expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately said yes when Jenna was stressed about her roommate in the rain and took on "her side work plus mine" so she could get home safely.

rationales.hedonism: He chose to cover the shift despite it being "busy but fine," putting his own comfort and ease aside rather than optimizing for relief or enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: After learning Tommy's grandpa died, he "sent him home" and covered the shift because "that's exactly what I should've done."
He had already "sent three emails" and joined a long call to try to get Priya promoted, then kept replaying the conversation because he felt he had "let her down."

rationales.conformity: He pushed back on the district manager's overtime complaint instead of simply accepting the directive, arguing that "my team has lives outside of work."
rationales.benevolence: He defended approving shift swaps because he was trying to accommodate his team's personal needs outside work.
What mattered most to him was that Jenna remembered his order and that the job can be "about people noticing each other," showing value placed on mutual care.

rationales.hedonism: He missed his own rest, meals, and sleep by staying two hours late, and later admits that saying yes is starting to feel like "failing myself."
rationales.security: He stayed specifically because "I wasn't going to make her walk home in the dark" and "It's not safe," acting to protect her from harm.
rationales.benevolence: He waited with Maria because she was "nineteen," stranded, and dealing with her mom's chemo bills, even though it was outside his job description.
He says "that's why I do it" after learning Priya is staying because "we treat people" well, showing that making coworkers feel seen is his central motivation.
After a long meeting she still spent the evening "helping Amma sort the kitchen," prioritizing support for a close family member.

rationales.universalism: She spent nearly an hour helping Fatima learn to write her name at a literacy camp and frames the difficult bus ride as worth it because of what that access means for a child beyond her immediate circle.
She values that a lesson about dirty water reached Ravi's family and calls it "a small thread pulling in the right direction," showing concern for broader public and environmental wellbeing.

rationales.benevolence: She "stayed back to help the peon uncle fix a broken desk," offering practical support to someone in her immediate school community.

rationales.conformity: She "kept quiet because Sharma sir is senior" and avoided challenging the room, prioritizing deference and social smoothness over confrontation.
rationales.universalism: She stayed silent while a senior teacher dismissed poor parents as people who don't value education, despite believing the comment was unfair to families working exhausting jobs.
She knowingly chose the convenient chain store instead of the local women's group whose sales "go directly back to the community," and calls the choice hypocritical against her fairness ideals.

rationales.self_direction: She resists the push toward a "better school" and tells Baba "I like my school," choosing the path that fits her own judgment rather than external status pressure.
rationales.universalism: She uses her unexpected free day to plan a unit on "water and sanitation" because she wants students carrying those conversations home, showing concern for wider community wellbeing.

rationales.hedonism: She gave up the restaurant dinner she had "been thinking about ... all week" and stayed on while exhausted and hungry, sacrificing immediate pleasure.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly anchors her choice in her mother's teaching: "when someone is drowning in grief, you throw them a rope," drawing on a handed-down moral norm.
rationales.benevolence: She stayed an extra forty-five minutes because the family was "alone" and leaving them in that state "felt wrong," offering direct care in an acute moment.

rationales.hedonism: Her planned Goa trip with "sit on the beach and eat fish curry and do absolutely nothing productive" is being threatened, showing a clear loss of pleasure and rest.
rationales.security: Being moved to nights after "five years of building my life around that schedule" and learning via WhatsApp shows her stable routine being disrupted rather than protected.

rationales.self_direction: She insists that "my life outside these walls is mine to plan," pushing back against work reorganizing her personal life through "someone else's spreadsheet."
rationales.hedonism: The shift change forces her to bargain away leisure, including the Goa trip and even a future Diwali holiday, undermining enjoyment and recovery.
rationales.security: She repeatedly emphasizes wanting "predictability," cites promised "schedule stability," and takes action to restore a workable routine by arranging a swap.

rationales.power: He fixates on being publicly cornered and wants the follow-up "in my building, at my table," showing a strong need to reassert authority and control.
rationales.benevolence: He treats Minh less as a partner than as someone who "ambush[ed] the person who signs your contracts," prioritizing dominance over preserving the other person's wellbeing.

rationales.power: He covertly sets the terms by booking the trip and withholding the real agenda: "Didn't mention the revenue share. Let him wonder."
rationales.benevolence: The deliberate choice to "let him wonder" uses uncertainty as leverage rather than engaging the other party in a considerate or supportive way.

rationales.achievement: He gets a concrete business result by pushing Minh from "twelve" to "a five-percent adjustment" and keeping the office profitable.
rationales.power: He is satisfied that he "steered the conversation" and made the concession "look generous," emphasizing command of the interaction and public deference.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes Minh "went along with it because he had to" and that he may now be "the last person he calls," showing he sacrificed trust for advantage.

rationales.power: It "felt good to be the one they came to" because of his reputation, which centers status and influence within his founder network.
rationales.benevolence: Being asked to "mentor their series B pitch" reflects a concrete willingness to help less experienced founders with something important to them.

rationales.power: He defends the school because of "the name and the network" and the doors it opens, framing his daughter's path through status and access.
rationales.security: He insists "the whole point of the school" is so she "never has to struggle the way I did," showing strong concern with protecting her future.
rationales.benevolence: He admits the choice may have been about "my idea of what her life should look like, not hers," and accepts being treated like a "service provider" as the cost of his plan.
He reaches out with a personal gesture, "texting her a photo of the dog sleeping on her old blanket," to reconnect and care for his daughter.

rationales.hedonism: After closing the deal, he says the dominant feeling is "not celebration" and ends the day too depleted to even "turn on the TV," showing pleasure was deferred or absent.
rationales.achievement: He endures "twelve-hour days" and holds firm through investor pressure until the extension is "Signed this afternoon," reflecting sustained pursuit of high performance.
He is absorbed by having "exceeded expectations on every metric" and by being noticed for the "regional director track," which is a classic success-and-recognition orientation.
rationales.power: He frames the review as "the kind of thing that gets you noticed" and protects his standing instead of sharing credit.
rationales.benevolence: He knowingly withholds Ahmed's contribution because he's "not about to split credit and make myself look like I need hand-holding," putting advancement over fairness to a subordinate.

rationales.hedonism: The whole entry centers present enjoyment and recovery through "Pool day," "Nobu Saturday night," and a "recovery brunch" after work stress.

rationales.power: He is preoccupied with being publicly questioned and insists that "if he has concerns, he can bring them to me privately," emphasizing rank and authority.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly leans on inherited rules from his father and other men he respected, saying "that's what being in charge looks like."
rationales.benevolence: "I'm not here to be liked. I'm here to deliver" signals a willingness to discount relational care for coworkers in favor of control and results.

rationales.hedonism: He pursues expensive immediate enjoyment through the "new Lebanese place on JBR" and considers still going out because "You only live once."
rationales.power: He chose the restaurant because he wanted "people to see us there" and savors the reaction when he paid, showing status-conscious display.
rationales.security: He knows "my account is going to be tight" and may "put it on the card and deal with it later," actively sacrificing financial stability.

rationales.power: He rejects cheaper living because "You can't network from a studio in Sharjah," prioritizing image and socially valuable connections.
rationales.security: His mother asks about "saving for a proper apartment," and he dismisses that long-term prudence in favor of status-oriented spending.

rationales.hedonism: He misses the night he wanted and ends up "eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights" instead of being out with friends, showing pleasure sacrificed to work strain.
rationales.achievement: He stays "until 10pm" to salvage the proposal and keep the company "still in the running," showing clear commitment to performance under pressure.

rationales.hedonism: He intentionally enjoys "a table by the pool at Sofitel, unlimited sparkling and eggs royale" and notes that by the third glass he was "actually relaxed."
rationales.power: He repeatedly ties the experience to being seen, calling photos and waitlists "evidence" that he's living the life he's supposed to be living.

rationales.achievement: Receiving "regional recognition for Q2 results" and immediately thinking about "what's next" shows an ongoing drive for external success.
rationales.power: The photo "with the CEO" and focus on recognition signal the importance of status and being visibly elevated.
He values the relationship partly because "She makes me look good. We look good together," making image and social positioning central.
rationales.benevolence: He repeatedly cancels, avoids the commitment conversation, and keeps her around despite knowing "Those are incompatible," which neglects her needs for clarity and care.

rationales.achievement: He still maps his future in terms of career milestones like "Regional director? Easy, if I keep this pace," showing a strong ladder-climbing mindset.
rationales.power: He evaluates lives through "status" and "visibility" and is disturbed by the idea of a life without those markers, even while questioning it.

rationales.achievement: He locks in a concrete win with the Trane contract after "Months of quoting and following up," reflecting persistence toward business success.
rationales.security: He values that the deal is "recurring revenue" and explicitly says "that's what matters right now," because it protects the business against uncertainty.
rationales.benevolence: The relief comes from knowing he can "keep the lights on for the guys who depend on me," tying the contract to caring for his employees.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the recruiter because he "built this business from one van and a credit card" and is not ready to give up the path he authored himself.
rationales.security: He repeatedly checks whether the "Numbers looked fine" and admits checking the bank balance "four times a day," showing strong concern with stability and financial protection.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses to drive out himself and spend "two hours up in the ceiling" because he values doing the work his own way rather than handing it off.
rationales.achievement: The entry centers competent problem-solving: he finds the fault nobody else caught and fixes it with "a twenty-dollar part" instead of overselling a replacement.

rationales.security: He evaluates hiring Dave by worrying what happens "if things slow down in October," showing careful concern with downside risk and business stability.
rationales.benevolence: He seriously weighs helping because otherwise he'd be "the guy who could've helped family and chose the spreadsheet instead," reflecting care for a close relative.

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately savors a simple present-moment reward: "I'm sitting on the deck with a beer and the yard looks good. That's enough for tonight."
rationales.achievement: "Everything got done" after multiple installs, service calls, and a supplier problem, signaling satisfaction in competent completion of hard work.
rationales.security: He highlights that "Everyone got paid" and that the truck is ready for tomorrow, emphasizing reliable continuity and practical stability.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the recruiter's pitch because "What I have now is smaller and harder but it's mine," clearly prioritizing independence over an imposed path.
rationales.power: He says the core issue is "someone else deciding what the net looks like," showing a strong need to retain control over his circumstances.
rationales.security: He explicitly turns away from corporate "stability" and benefits because control matters more, which conflicts with prioritizing the safest or most predictable option.

rationales.achievement: She says her "own print series hasn't moved since July" because she has become the person "who manages everyone's access," showing her own artistic progress being sidelined.
rationales.conformity: She spent "about forty minutes" mediating the press dispute and proposed a split arrangement so "they both agreed," prioritizing smooth relations and group norms.
rationales.benevolence: She invested time resolving Sylvain and Oriane's conflict instead of focusing on herself, helping hold the collective together.

rationales.self_direction: She draws a firm boundary around her autonomy with "The trick is not letting it eat into the morning. Mornings are mine."
rationales.achievement: She made concrete progress on the canvas, saying she "finally found the right temperature for the water," and she also pushed through the admin efficiently.
She has been considering the prize "for two months" but "haven't even started mine," showing avoidance of a concrete professional goal.
rationales.benevolence: She said yes to reviewing Oriane's statement and spent "twenty minutes editing her paragraphs," giving useful support to a peer.

rationales.achievement: She breaks the application into concrete steps: "start the statement this weekend, pick the images, and get decent photographs," showing active movement toward the prize.

rationales.hedonism: Even though "it's late and I should sleep," she chooses more work instead of rest or comfort.
rationales.achievement: She commits to setting "the alarm for six" to work on the statement before anyone else arrives, prioritizing progress toward the application.

rationales.self_direction: She gives up her own plan because "you don't say no to Sunday lunch. You just don't," letting family expectation override her choice.
rationales.achievement: She "got up at six" and drafted the statement, revising until she had something "decent," which is clear effort toward the application.
rationales.conformity: Her immediate yes to lunch comes from a rule-like expectation rather than preference, showing deference to family norms.

rationales.achievement: She admits the role lets her matter "in a way that doesn't depend on whether my paintings are good enough," and says that is "the one question I keep avoiding."
rationales.power: She recognizes that when she manages things, "I'm in charge" and "People defer to my schedule," showing clear satisfaction in influence and control.

rationales.achievement: The entry centers on feeling she still doesn't "add up to enough" despite all her responsibility, framing her actions against external standards of success.
rationales.conformity: She lists being the one who "iron[s] my clothes, pay[s] bills on time" and keeps the collective orderly, emphasizing disciplined, socially acceptable conduct.

rationales.achievement: She completed key submission tasks: "Finished the artist statement," photographed all ten pieces, and got the application drafted down to "Just need to press send."
She already "Submitted the Prix Dauphine application," which is a concrete act of pursuing recognition for her work.
rationales.security: She says it felt "safer to be the person who organises things than the person who wants things," explicitly choosing the less vulnerable, more protected role.

rationales.tradition: He values that the glaze came out "exactly as Master Kwon taught me" and shares the piece with his mother in a moment tied to family and lineage.
He resists the digital collaboration because it would turn the pieces into "screens," and he weighs the proposal through what "Master Kwon say about it," grounding the decision in inherited practice.
He joins the festival planning board linked to his lineage, noting his father and teacher served before him and that sitting there felt like "slotting into a space that was kept warm for me."
He is troubled by Eunji's children "barely speaking Korean" and by her having "folded into something else entirely," showing attachment to cultural continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He stays because "Someone needs to be here for Mother," prioritizing a close family obligation over visiting his sister.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to reshape his work for the market, saying "I smiled and showed him the door" and "My kiln is not a factory floor."
rationales.tradition: He rejects making the work "smaller, simpler, pastel glazes" because that would dilute the craft into something "unrecognizable," preserving established standards of the work.
He dismisses adopting Christmas as unnecessary because "We do Seollal and Chuseok. That has always been enough," explicitly affirming inherited observances.
He follows "the same schedule my teacher kept, same schedule his teacher kept" for the first firing, and the whole scene is framed as grounding ritual and continuity.

rationales.conformity: He restrains the impulse to correct the hanbok because "She would have felt bad" and decides "It does not matter," prioritizing social harmony over criticism.
rationales.benevolence: He withholds the correction to protect Eunji's feelings and centers that "The kids looked happy," showing care for close family members.

rationales.achievement: He spent the afternoon comparing his draft to papers in "top journals" and framed the delay around whether the sacrifice was yielding "visible progress," showing strong investment in excelling by external standards.
rationales.security: The choice has already cost them "a good apartment, a pension plan" and now may mean being "another month behind schedule," showing active movement away from stability and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: He told Ingrid "fine" even though she was "picking up extra client work" and he knew she "looked tired," withholding something important instead of supporting the relationship openly.

rationales.achievement: "Revised twenty-three pages of the literature review" reflects sustained effort toward a demanding academic standard rather than coasting.
He is still oriented around completing a major professional goal, describing work he has been pursuing for "eighteen months" and worrying about whether he can still "build something."
rationales.security: He contrasts his situation with people who can "afford to fail," mentions his wife buying cheaper groceries, and longs for "predictable paychecks," showing his path is undermining financial stability.

rationales.hedonism: He "walked home the long way," watched the boats, and "felt okay about being here," showing present-moment enjoyment rather than pure productivity.
rationales.benevolence: He stayed after class to walk Willem through the model "step by step" until the student's face shifted "from furrowed to open," directly investing effort in someone else's learning.

rationales.security: He "ran the numbers" on savings, worries about funding by September, and has started browsing finance jobs "just in case," all of which show active prioritization of fallback stability.

rationales.achievement: His reaction centers on rigor and performance standards, noting the other student's work was "sloppy" while he previously spent "three weeks revising" after pointed methodological criticism.

rationales.security: Telling Ingrid about the "runway calculations" and hearing the "worst case scenario" acknowledged made the situation feel "lighter," showing movement toward greater stability through direct reality-checking.
rationales.benevolence: He finally "told her everything," and the entry centers on mutual support: they are "still sitting here, still together, still figuring it out."

rationales.self_direction: She says the version of herself who doesn't help "feels like a stranger" and wonders if she gives because she's "scared of who I'd be if I stopped," suggesting she is not acting from freely chosen boundaries.
rationales.security: She keeps lending money she may not get back and says she'd rather be "broke" than keep more for herself, actively sacrificing her own financial protection.
rationales.benevolence: She lends money for the girls' school shoes, drives "40 minutes" to help her aunt, and says she'd rather be broke than fail to "show up for people."

rationales.hedonism: After "twelve hours of driving," she uses her break to pick up medication for her aunt and ends with "Just want to sleep," showing pleasure and rest were deferred.
rationales.benevolence: Even during an exhausting workday, she picked up "tia Elena's medication" on her break to help a close family member.
The moment is moving because of prior care for family: Diego repaid her, the girls wanted to give their "piggy bank money," and she told them to keep it.

rationales.tradition: She spent the afternoon making holiday tamales with her aunt using "her mother's recipe, the one with the olives," clearly continuing a family custom.
rationales.benevolence: The day is centered on caring connection with a close relative, spending "the whole afternoon together, just us and the masa."

rationales.security: She plans to "pick up extra shifts" and "skip the new tires for another month," putting her own financial and practical safety at risk.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately frames helping as non-optional: "He's my little brother; that's just what you do," and is willing to rearrange her life to support him.

rationales.security: Her focus is on preventing future harm, worrying "what if next time it's worse?" and considering whether her aunt should move in so she is not alone.
rationales.benevolence: She feels guilty she "wasn't there" and is thinking about having her aunt move in, showing strong concern for a close family member's wellbeing.

rationales.hedonism: She says taking care of herself feels "selfish," has been "running on empty for months," and cannot remember the last time she relaxed without guilt.
rationales.tradition: The gathering includes "the grape thing at midnight," family food, and holiday rituals shared across generations, showing active participation in inherited custom.
rationales.benevolence: Watching everyone eat her food and laugh, she thinks, "this is why I do everything," explicitly locating fulfillment in caring for her people.

rationales.self_direction: She had chosen the coffee ritual for herself but said yes to Diego "before I even finished reading," then admitted even finishing her coffee would have felt bad, showing difficulty holding her own plan.
rationales.hedonism: A tiny pleasure she had intentionally set aside for herself lasted only "five minutes," and she treats even finishing it as suspect rather than acceptable enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately agrees to watch "the girls after school" and reframes the interruption as "not a sacrifice," showing readiness to prioritize close others.

rationales.hedonism: He got home late again because he stayed to help Katja, and Brigitte had already eaten; present comfort and shared leisure were sacrificed to work.
rationales.achievement: "My department scored highest" and the satisfaction that "the numbers confirm what you feel in the classroom" show strong investment in measurable professional excellence.
rationales.benevolence: He "stayed an extra forty minutes" helping Katja with a parent complaint, giving time and care to support a colleague.

rationales.achievement: He fixates on not being nominated, checks whether he is "slipping," and points to "solid" numbers, showing concern with recognized competence and standing.
rationales.power: The sting comes from status allocation: "nobody thought to put my name forward," and he is preoccupied with who gets visible prestige and whose work gets seen.

rationales.achievement: He "hadn't touched" his own conference paper and admits his evenings keep going to others' problems, actively sidelining his own accomplishment goal.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately agrees to review Leon's rubric and help Simone with parents, repeatedly giving his time to support colleagues.
He forgot to call his sister on her birthday and realized it was "another thing I let slide because the school day swallowed everything," showing neglect of close relationships.

rationales.hedonism: He "managed the whole afternoon without opening the laptop," took a scenic walk with Brigitte, and described it as "coming back to something," clearly prioritizing enjoyment and restoration.
rationales.benevolence: He centers both his time with Brigitte and his sense that "those three depend on me," showing sustained care for close others even while weighing competing demands.

rationales.conformity: He says "Everyone clapped at the staff meeting and I clapped too" even though it felt "mechanical," restraining his true feelings to follow the expected social response.

rationales.achievement: He spends the evening on Leon's rubric "instead of the conference paper again," and admits he can see the pattern but not interrupt it, undermining his own advancement goal.
rationales.benevolence: Reviewing Leon's rubric instead of his own paper is another direct choice to support a colleague with his time.

rationales.achievement: He requests to attend a major conference and is affirmed as "long overdue for professional development," which is a clear step toward growth and professional recognition.
rationales.benevolence: He called Hilde "properly this time, no agenda, just to talk" and was more present with her, showing care for a close family relationship.

rationales.achievement: He wrote "two decent pages," found the argument flowing, and treated closing the laptop before midnight as a "small accomplishment," all signaling purposeful progress on his paper.
rationales.benevolence: Brigitte brings tea, reads his draft, and he receives her affirmation with care, making this an engaged, present interaction with a close partner rather than another absent evening.

rationales.self_direction: He finally told the group he needed to "scale back for the next two weeks" and discovered that "nobody collapsed," asserting his own boundaries against an imagined obligation.
rationales.achievement: He scales back others' demands specifically for the "paper deadline," then returns to the draft and sees "the structure is taking shape," showing a deliberate turn toward his own work.

rationales.benevolence: Brigitte has "stopped inviting the Fischers" because he cancels or is "half-present," and he admits he repeatedly chooses measurable work over simply being there for loved ones.

rationales.achievement: He endures a painful late return and plans to go back because "the deal isn't done," reflecting persistence toward business performance.
rationales.security: He is pulled back immediately by contract instability and a looming "client audit on Friday," focusing on protecting operational stability and reducing risk.
rationales.tradition: "The evening puja helped settle my mind" shows an active return to an established religious practice for grounding.
He frames the company through lineage, from his father's shop to the hope that Aarav would "take it further," showing strong concern with continuity across generations.
rationales.benevolence: His attention is on Aarav, Maa, and the emotional meaning of who carries things forward, reflecting care for close family rather than detached business analysis alone.

rationales.self_direction: He recognizes that Aarav may need to build something "on his own terms," explicitly making room for an independent path instead of insisting on inheritance.
rationales.hedonism: He returns to Maa's, savors that "the dal tasted exactly right," and lets that sensory comfort be "enough for one day."
rationales.achievement: Even late at night he sits with the Q1 numbers and strategizes about sourcing, staying engaged with business performance and problem-solving.
rationales.security: He is preoccupied with margin pressure, supplier leverage, and the need to find "a second source," all aimed at protecting the business from vulnerability.
rationales.tradition: The repeated focus on Maa's table, Baba's preferred dal, and "the same steel plates we've used since I was a boy" shows deep grounding in inherited family practice.
rationales.benevolence: He goes back to Maa's without being asked and reflects tenderly on Aarav being far away, centering close family bonds.

rationales.achievement: He compares his firm against a peer's headline success and defends his own record of employing 200 people and never missing deliveries, showing strong focus on accomplishment metrics.
rationales.power: The Business Standard profile and thoughts about what looks "more impressive" reveal concern with prestige, visibility, and rank.
rationales.tradition: He contrasts his current scorekeeping with his father's emphasis on "quality" and long-term customers, using inherited standards as a moral reference point.
rationales.benevolence: He admits he wondered whether Aarav would have stayed if the company were bigger and also failed to call Shobha, both showing close relationships being subordinated to business/status thinking.

rationales.hedonism: He explicitly wants the stillness of Varanasi but refuses the full week because of work, choosing duty over rest and pleasure.
rationales.achievement: He refuses to hand off a "forty-lakh capital commitment" and continues managing supplier calls and interviews, prioritizing business execution.
rationales.security: The refusal to step away is tied to controlling a major investment and limiting business risk around a consequential decision.
rationales.tradition: He is drawn to "the aarti," the ghats, and the extended family gathering, showing active attachment to inherited religious and family practices.
rationales.benevolence: Shobha goes quiet after he cuts the trip short, and he knowingly deprioritizes time with family despite wanting to be there.

rationales.achievement: He signs off on the investment and says, "It's the right call" and "the work is the clearest thing I have," showing strong alignment with decisive business accomplishment.
rationales.tradition: He justifies himself through his father's example of working "six days a week until he was seventy-two," treating inherited provider norms as authoritative.
rationales.benevolence: He dismisses family concern about the shortened trip as if others are making it a test, minimizing their desire for his presence.

rationales.achievement: He is energized by the product taking form: "These are the moments I still like about this work, when something you decided on paper becomes real in your hands."
rationales.tradition: Aarav keeping "Maa's old brass diyas" and his desire to continue links between Patil's father and his own father show strong attachment to inherited continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He closely attends to Aarav's apartment photo and the diya he kept, showing warm emotional investment in a close family bond.

rationales.achievement: He keeps setting targets for the biodegradable line, including reaching spec by mid-April and getting samples to pilot customers, which shows active goal-driven execution.
rationales.tradition: His strongest emotional response comes from Aarav finding "his own version" of going to temple after seeing him and Maa do it, signaling the value of inherited practice.
rationales.benevolence: He rearranges work to arrive early for the trip, calls Aarav just to hear his voice, and is moved by what has been passed on within the family.

rationales.hedonism: He relishes simple pleasure and relief in Varanasi: family meals, sleep, morning walks, and the discovery that he had forgotten what it felt like to stop solving problems.
rationales.tradition: The entry centers on "the aarti," temple bells, and the meaning of being embedded in extended family roles, showing strong alignment with established familial and religious ways.
rationales.benevolence: He values being "someone's son-in-law, someone's uncle" and spends the trip immersed in family presence rather than productivity.

rationales.hedonism: He rejects Shobha's request to go to Varanasi more often because stepping away from work makes him tighten up, choosing duty over rest and enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He prioritizes the business and celebrates that the fourth trial "hit target spec," reinforcing a strong focus on successful execution.
rationales.power: He accepts "the board seat," taking on a visible position of influence and leadership in the industry association.
rationales.tradition: He frames the board seat through long family history, noting that "His father and mine started in the same market forty years ago," and treats that continuity as morally important.
rationales.benevolence: He wants to "help the next group of small manufacturers," gives his team a day off after success, and shares the milestone with Aarav, showing active care toward close and near-community relationships.

rationales.self_direction: She "kept waiting for someone else to flag something" and "just nodded along" instead of acting on her own judgment, suppressing her own voice.
rationales.security: She explains that she spent years making sure everything ran "without friction" and feared creating "surprises" or "awkward moments," showing a strong pull toward safety and predictability.
rationales.conformity: She avoided raising her hand because asking might "slow everyone down" and "make a mess someone else has to clean up," prioritizing smooth social functioning over speaking up.
rationales.benevolence: Her concern was not just embarrassment but avoiding extra burden on others, framing her silence around not creating work or discomfort for other people.

rationales.self_direction: She "wanted to say this is not what we decided" but instead acted "like I didn't have an opinion at all," going against independent self-expression.
rationales.conformity: She stayed agreeable because she "didn't want to be the one who drags things backward" and smiled along with the group despite disagreeing.

rationales.self_direction: She describes "biting the inside of your cheek because you disagree" and still not saying it aloud, showing restraint of her own viewpoint rather than independent action.
rationales.conformity: She frames her habit as "reading the room" and "knowing when to let something go," treating harmony and avoiding tension as the right course.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly contrasts American assertiveness with "I learned different things" and affirms the older clinic-based way she spent "a lifetime building," grounding herself in inherited norms.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers on small pleasures like walking home in the "gold" light and eating "a pan dulce" on a bench, actively savoring comfort in the moment.
rationales.achievement: She "spent the afternoon in the library finishing the epidemiology reading" and works through the textbook "slowly, one section at a time," showing disciplined academic effort.
rationales.security: Patricia's text about the grandchildren and Sunday dinner "settled something in me I didn't know was unsettled," signaling comfort in stable, reassuring family ties.
rationales.tradition: References to "Miguel from the parish," pan dulce, and making "abuela's arroz con pollo" tie her sense of place to familiar cultural and communal practices.
rationales.benevolence: Her emotional center lands on the family message that the kids are better and want her cooking, showing warmth and investment in close family relationships.

rationales.security: She contrasts the uncertainty of school with the comforting feeling that "the rhythms I already know are enough," emphasizing grounding and stability.
rationales.tradition: A long family dinner with arroz con pollo and familiar hosting rituals becomes the frame through which she understands herself, showing continuity with established ways.
rationales.benevolence: The entry centers on feeding and gathering with Patricia, the kids, Miguel, and Estela, finding meaning in caring for and being with her close circle.

rationales.achievement: She spends "six hours on the commission piece" for a major hotel client, repeatedly revising the canvas to get it right, which reflects sustained performance-oriented effort.
rationales.benevolence: Even after exhausting studio work, she goes to the center to organize supplies and buy more paint for the kids, investing energy and money into supporting them.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I knew exactly what I wanted to say" but "didn't say a single thing," suppressing her own convictions instead of acting on them.
rationales.conformity: She admits she "didn't want the tension" and didn't want her aunt looking at her like she "make things difficult," so she chose social ease over dissent.
rationales.universalism: Her unspoken defense of the "Sudanese workers" and insistence that displaced people deserve dignity shows clear concern for justice beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.benevolence: She notices Ahmad's progress, gently encourages him with "the yellow was a good choice," and values that the space helped him grow on his own terms.

rationales.hedonism: She chose the sable brushes because "Just once I wanted something nice for myself," explicitly prioritizing personal pleasure and comfort.
rationales.benevolence: She says "The kids need materials more than I need sable brushes" and "I did it anyway," acknowledging that she chose against their needs.

rationales.self_direction: She says the joke "landed wrong" but still "just laughed along," acting against her own immediate moral reaction.
rationales.conformity: Laughing along with Dina instead of objecting shows she followed the social moment rather than risking friction.
rationales.universalism: Joining in after a joke that cats "should all be rounded up" goes against broader concern for vulnerable beings beyond her close circle.

rationales.benevolence: She brings her prized brushes to the center because she kept thinking about "fourteen kids sharing broken-handled acrylics" and concludes they "belong where they get used."

rationales.self_direction: He leaves a secure senior role despite others questioning it, choosing his own path rather than the conventional one others expect.
rationales.stimulation: He says "something just goes dead inside me when I know exactly what the next six months will look like," showing a strong need to escape predictability.
rationales.security: Walking away from a "senior role with stock options" because predictability feels deadening means sacrificing stability and assurance.

rationales.stimulation: A new stack, a fast-moving CTO, and an office that feels "either exhilarating or exhausting" clearly attract him because the environment is novel and intense.
rationales.achievement: He stays up "past midnight going through documentation," showing strong effort to perform well in the new role.
rationales.security: He admits the move was not really lateral and that he "took a pay cut," trading financial stability for the new opportunity.

rationales.achievement: He immediately notes being given "ownership of the payment reconciliation service on day four," treating early responsibility as a meaningful marker of competence and progress.
He admits he wants to "impress" Farah, measures himself against "everyone in the room," and even screenshots Samir's praise because being recognized for strong performance matters to him.

rationales.stimulation: Saying yes immediately to a public talk before thinking it through shows attraction to challenge and high-intensity novelty.
rationales.achievement: He wants to be "the one who ships and talks about it and gets known," directly tying the decision to competence and visible accomplishment.
rationales.power: The appeal is partly status-based: he wants to be recognized publicly and seen as "the kind of person who does these things."

rationales.self_direction: He rejects Walid's model of staying put for fifteen years and defends his own choice to move, prioritizing a self-authored path over others' expectations.
rationales.stimulation: He frames the criticism as coming from someone who "can't imagine choosing otherwise," implying that choosing change and movement is central to him.
rationales.security: He dismisses the safer long-term stability represented by the same bank for fifteen years, favoring uncertainty instead.

rationales.stimulation: He keeps getting pulled toward "a new Kubernetes operator" and a side-project CLI idea, showing a strong pull toward novelty over routine preparation.
rationales.achievement: He knows the talk "would be good for visibility" but keeps avoiding the slides, undermining a concrete opportunity for performance and recognition.
He spends "two hours" refining the self-assessment, catalogs his deliverables, and admits "I do need" others to see him, showing a strong investment in recognized accomplishment.

rationales.hedonism: After the talk he drives with "the windows down," puts on Fairuz, and enjoys the mood of the evening, actively savoring the good feeling.
rationales.achievement: He highlights that the demo worked, people asked for his LinkedIn, and Samir mentioned it in the team channel, centering the success and recognition of the performance.

rationales.stimulation: He becomes uneasy as work turns familiar and immediately fixates on "what else is out there," even bookmarking a job in a "completely different domain."
rationales.achievement: The tech lead path is described as "a big deal" with "more responsibility, more visibility, a path to VP," showing that advancement and visible success strongly register for him.
rationales.security: Even though he says "I have a good thing here," he is pulled toward another risky move, undermining stability and continuity.

rationales.stimulation: He says "the looking feels like breathing — I don't know how to stop," making the pursuit of the next possibility feel essential.
rationales.security: He recognizes the family has already changed schools "twice in four years because of me," showing that his pattern creates instability.
rationales.benevolence: He knows Dina is "tired of it" and that the repeated searching burdens his wife and kids, yet he keeps feeding the pattern anyway.

rationales.stimulation: He says he is "running toward the next thing because it makes me feel alive" and that routine makes him "start suffocating," showing a strong need for novelty and intensity.
rationales.achievement: The offer appeals because it is "everything that would look right on a CV" and fits a path of building, getting recognized, and proving himself.
rationales.security: He describes a repeated cycle of leaving once things settle and is tempted away from a stable leadership track because it feels like "a cage," rejecting predictability.

rationales.achievement: She "presented the new curriculum framework" she developed and was chosen to lead implementation, showing recognized competence and strong performance.
rationales.power: Saying "it feels right to be the one steering this" and insisting "someone capable needed to do it" shows comfort with directing others and holding authority.

rationales.hedonism: She endured physical discomfort, skipped dinner, and kept working because she "couldn't leave it to anyone else," prioritizing work over present comfort and enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: Spending "four extra hours at school" on the implementation timeline and focusing on proving she can "execute better than anyone else" reflects strong drive to excel.
rationales.power: Her fixation on leading the project and being the most capable person in the room shows a clear desire for control and influence.
rationales.tradition: She dismisses seniority outright with "seniority doesn't mean capability," rejecting an established basis for status and deference.
rationales.benevolence: She brushes off her mother's concern and frames missing dinner as acceptable because work mattered more, putting close relationships behind her professional aims.

rationales.self_direction: She resists others' pressure to soften her approach, insisting she will not "lower standards just to make everyone feel included."
rationales.achievement: Rewriting weak drafts and focusing on "results" over comfort shows a strong commitment to high standards and effective performance.
rationales.power: Rewriting three people's work without consulting them and rejecting shared ownership shows a preference for taking charge and controlling outcomes.
rationales.conformity: She openly dismisses "buy-in" and others' feelings, and acts without consultation, which conflicts with maintaining harmony and expected collaborative norms.
rationales.benevolence: Her response centers on not having time for others' feelings and treating colleagues' ownership as secondary, showing disregard for close others' needs.

rationales.achievement: The "revised timeline" was approved "without changes," and she prepared the meeting with required reading, showing effective, goal-focused execution.
rationales.power: She "pre-assigned all discussion topics to keep things focused," which shows she is structuring others' participation from a position of control.

rationales.achievement: She takes pride in having earned "that title" and in the fact that her recommendation letters now "carry more weight," reflecting a strong concern with recognized effectiveness.
rationales.power: She values that "when I speak in staff meetings, people listen differently" and that her ideas get implemented, showing a clear orientation toward influence and formal authority.
rationales.benevolence: She connects her position directly to helping a student, saying "Arjun got into his school because I was in a position to help him effectively," which shows concrete support for someone in her care.

rationales.hedonism: She says she just wants to "curl up with a book that isn't required reading," prioritizing immediate comfort and enjoyment over career-building tasks.
rationales.achievement: She knows she "should be building my CV" but is pulling away from that push, showing active disengagement from advancement-oriented striving.

rationales.hedonism: She savors "morning coffee" in a new kissaten and spends an hour "watching the light change," emphasizing sensory pleasure and a leisurely experience.

rationales.self_direction: She shifts from chasing what she is "supposed to want" to asking "whether I actually want the things I'm chasing," showing independent choice-making.
rationales.hedonism: She decides "this summer I want to actually enjoy" and evaluates the option by "what feels good," clearly prioritizing lived enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She declines a "prestigious, competitive" opportunity because the status payoff is not worth the cost, rejecting externally impressive advancement.

rationales.hedonism: She "just wanted to enjoy the tonkatsu" but could "barely taste any of it" while defending her life choices, showing present pleasure being overridden rather than pursued.

rationales.self_direction: She deliberately chooses things because she wants them, noting the bookshelf project was "completely pointless from a productivity standpoint and that's exactly why it felt good."
rationales.hedonism: Reorganizing books for beauty, cooking dinner from scratch, and enjoying an "ordinary evening" show deliberate investment in comfort and pleasure.
rationales.achievement: She explicitly turns away from productive work with "could have been writing" and embraces an activity that is "pointless from a productivity standpoint."

rationales.hedonism: The happiest part of her week was making hot chocolate and watching a cooking show, a small act of comfort she genuinely enjoyed.
rationales.achievement: She submitted only because it "wasn't optional" and describes the work as "hollow," signaling active alienation from performance and prestige metrics.
rationales.conformity: She submits the abstract because her advisor made clear it "wasn't optional," showing compliance with external expectations despite her reluctance.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the inherited script that pleasure must be earned and says "I don't want that to be my story," asserting her own way of living.
rationales.hedonism: She insists on being able to "have a nice dinner or watch a movie without guilt," defending present enjoyment as something valuable in itself.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly pushes back on the East Asian academic norm of "work hard, sacrifice now, enjoy later," rejecting a deeply inherited cultural pattern.

rationales.hedonism: She describes "warm food, easy conversation, no agenda" as the kind of evening she actually enjoys and defends keeping space for that.
rationales.achievement: She criticizes the idea that people must give up "hobbies, relationships, sleep" for a slightly better career outcome, pushing back on success-through-suffering.
rationales.benevolence: Her sadness that peers have "given up hobbies, relationships, sleep" shows genuine concern for the wellbeing of people around her.

rationales.self_direction: She wants to prove she can do something nice for herself with "no celebration, no milestone, just a Wednesday," resisting the need for external justification.
rationales.hedonism: She spends freely on a "nice dinner" and "the wine pairing" specifically to enjoy herself, treating pleasure as sufficient reason.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her advisor she wants a career "but not at the cost of actually living," asserting her own terms instead of accepting the default path.
rationales.hedonism: Ending the night with a long walk, yellow ginkgo trees, and warm taiyaki reflects an intentional appreciation of ordinary pleasure alongside work.
rationales.achievement: She explicitly rejects "decades of relentless output" as a model to emulate, pushing back on maximizing career performance above all else.

rationales.stimulation: She prefers mapped-out routines, standardized schedules, and steady savings progress, showing clear avoidance of novelty and uncertainty.
rationales.achievement: She treats the strong review and renewal track as important feedback and immediately organizes the semester in a highly performance-focused way.
rationales.security: Hearing she is "on track for contract renewal," mapping plans through December, and updating a down payment spreadsheet all center on stability and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: The shared savings planning with Miguel toward an apartment goal shows practical care for a close relationship and their joint future.

rationales.self_direction: She insists "my choices make sense for my life, even if they don't make sense to her," showing she is choosing based on her own judgment rather than pressure.
rationales.stimulation: She turns down an "amazing experience" abroad because she does not want the uncertainty of starting over, clearly avoiding novelty and disruption.
rationales.security: Her reasoning centers on "no guarantee of renewal," wanting her mother nearby, seniority, and pension contributions, all of which prioritize stability and protection.

rationales.self_direction: She is actively re-examining her own assumptions, admitting "maybe I'm holding onto an idea of permanence that doesn't really exist," which shows reflective independent thinking.
rationales.security: She recognizes she has "built everything around wanting things to stay the same," revealing a strong orientation toward permanence and stability even as she questions it.

rationales.achievement: He treats the supervisor's comments as actionable progress, saying the feedback was useful and that he "should focus on that."
rationales.tradition: The recurring Tuesday call with Mama and the familiar question about coming home show ongoing orientation toward family ties and expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He checks in with Mama and redirects the conversation to "Noor's day at the shop," keeping close family concerns in view.

rationales.hedonism: He has had "three nights of maybe four hours' sleep" and has been "eating rice and lentils for two weeks straight," showing ongoing self-denial rather than present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: The entry centers on finishing the conference paper, worrying about falling behind, and comparing himself to a peer with "two journal submissions this year."
rationales.security: He is financially strained, academically behind, and spiraling over his supervisor's message, with uncertainty feeling hard to contain.
rationales.tradition: His sense of duty to support Noor and stay connected to Papa's care reflects strong family obligation rather than individual convenience.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Noor "I'd figure it out" and hides his own hardship because "She doesn't need that," prioritizing his family's burden over his own.

rationales.achievement: He is relieved that the paper can be made "submittable by July" and takes the narrowed scope as concrete progress on the work.
rationales.security: The supervisor's clarification and Noor finding "a cheaper repair guy" both reduce uncertainty, and he notes that his "shoulders drop" with the relief.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately shares the good meeting news with Mama and stays emotionally invested in Noor's repair problem.

rationales.hedonism: After the presentation he "couldn't eat dinner," and the whole entry is dominated by pressure rather than enjoyment or comfort.
rationales.achievement: He rehearsed four times, cared that his slides were clean and data organized, and keeps a spreadsheet tracking goals like publication and exams.
rationales.security: He frames the degree as having "traded one kind of pressure for another" that "pays nothing," showing the pursuit has made his life feel less stable.
rationales.tradition: What 'everything' meant was his "mother watching from the front row" and his father understanding the sacrifice, tying the effort to family recognition and continuity.

rationales.achievement: He submitted the revised paper, notes that it "reads much better," and is systematically moving the teaching assistantship application toward completion.

rationales.self_direction: He insists "I didn't leave a perfectly good job to do a watered-down version" and frames choosing the harder theoretical path as a deliberate decision.
rationales.achievement: He wants "the harder version," is pushing through the comprehensive reading list, and measures himself against a demanding academic standard.
rationales.security: The comment about maybe not being able to close the gap and only "barely" keeping pace shows the path feels precarious and threatening.

rationales.stimulation: He pushes himself through the chapter that had been "haunting" him and then goes to the departmental social he had been avoiding, engaging challenge and novelty instead of staying in routine.
rationales.hedonism: He describes it as a "Good day. Actually good," enjoys the conversation at the social, and notices the walk home and smell of rain with clear pleasure.
rationales.achievement: Working through three practice problems and caring that the third error was "not a conceptual one" shows strong investment in mastering difficult material.
rationales.benevolence: He warmly shares the social update with Mama and treasures Noor's photo enough to save it to his home screen.

rationales.achievement: Getting the TA role after semesters of tracking deadlines becomes a concrete sign that "you belong here" and that the work is paying off.
rationales.security: He values that the stipend means he can send Noor more "without skipping meals myself," making his situation materially steadier.
rationales.benevolence: He says the money matters because he can support Noor more, putting family care ahead of the CV benefit.

rationales.achievement: The TA position matters because it proves "I'm not wasting this" and that the years and money spent produced a recognizable accomplishment.
rationales.tradition: What he most wants is his father knowing the sacrifice "turned into something he can recognize," grounding the success in parental approval and family meaning.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Mama to share the news and measures its significance through what it means to his parents, especially Papa.

rationales.power: She feels displaced when Priya books the venue "without asking me," and then does not assert herself despite feeling reduced to someone merely informed afterward.
rationales.conformity: She stays silent because she "didn't want to be the difficult one again," restraining herself to avoid conflict and social friction.

rationales.power: She is frustrated by being forced to wait for others to decide "when my house gets fixed," experiencing the whole afternoon as a loss of control over her own household.
She takes satisfaction in the fish vendor knowing her standards and in the cupboard staying "exactly as I organize it," emphasizing command over her domestic environment.
rationales.tradition: The day revolves around recurring family and cultural routines like making dosa, taking the grandchildren to Tamil tuition, and preserving old household order.
rationales.benevolence: She cooks for everyone, transports the grandchildren, and organizes family keepsakes, showing active care for close relatives.
She spends the evening helping Arjun with his geography project, and his "Paati knows everything" comment lands because the care is direct and close.

rationales.power: She realizes "The conversation wasn't a discussion. It was a notification dressed up as one," showing her authority in the family decision was bypassed.
rationales.security: She argues for the current school because it is "perfectly fine" and grounded in known teachers and stable fundamentals rather than a flashy unknown option.
rationales.tradition: Her defense of "the same one Vikram attended" and skepticism toward the new school reflects preference for established family ways over change.

rationales.power: She directly says "I told her to stop" and insists Kavitha does not get to rearrange her house, clearly reasserting authority over the space.
rationales.tradition: The pooja shelf contains sacred and inherited items like "the brass Ganesha that my mother gave me," and she restores the arrangement she has maintained for thirty-five years.

rationales.power: Finding out through "A WhatsApp message" and being told she was making it too big a deal leaves her excluded from an important family decision.
rationales.conformity: She deletes both messages because Priya would read them and it would "become a whole thing," suppressing open confrontation to contain discord.
rationales.tradition: She wants to be told "face to face" at the dining table, showing attachment to more traditional forms of family respect and consultation.
rationales.benevolence: Her distress centers on what it means for "my grandson" to no longer be nearby, showing strong concern for close family bonds.

rationales.power: She explicitly tells Priya that "household staff coordination goes through me" and spends the morning instructing the maid on how things are done.
rationales.tradition: The disruption hits during her "morning prayers," and her instructions about the kolam powder and household order reflect attachment to established domestic and cultural practice.

rationales.power: When the driver tries to overcharge her, she pushes back with "I've been taking this route for ten years, I know the rate," asserting control rather than accepting his terms.

rationales.self_direction: He followed his own instincts, saying he "just kept walking" and followed a stranger through an alley to find a hidden spot instead of sticking to the usual path.
rationales.stimulation: He explicitly says he was "bored" with the main spots and chased an unlisted basement bar hidden behind a laundromat, showing a clear draw toward novelty and surprise.
rationales.achievement: He spent the evening updating a pitch deck and worrying about how to make the "growth story more compelling" before an investor meeting, showing active pursuit of startup success.
rationales.security: He took a red-eye between cities, booked a hostel for networking, and kept chasing uncertain partnerships, favoring movement and opportunity over stability.

rationales.self_direction: He defends his own path against family pressure, saying her version of "okay" feels like "suffocation" and that he wants to be "free."
rationales.stimulation: He rejects a life that would make him "comfortable enough that you stop looking for what else is out there," framing constant exploration as essential.
rationales.security: He dismisses the steady paycheck and stable career his mother wants, calling salary "the trap" rather than something to seek.
rationales.conformity: He resists the expected respectable path of titles and steady work, saying he is tired of defending choices that feel obvious to him.
rationales.tradition: The entry pushes back against his family's conventional script for adulthood and success, especially the pressure to match a classmate's respectable corporate path.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses to surround himself with people who validate risk-taking and says "the risk is the point," emphasizing a self-authored life over safer norms.
rationales.stimulation: He celebrates hopping rooftop bars with founders across cities and says "at least we'll know we tried something different," showing clear attraction to novelty and challenge.
rationales.security: He explicitly rejects stability, noting that none of them has stable jobs and that "you don't build something new by playing it safe."
rationales.benevolence: He distances himself from lasting close ties, calling others merely "fellow travelers" and saying "I'm not looking for permanence," which downplays commitment to close, enduring relationships.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects pressure from investors and his co-founder to build something safer, saying if he wanted to build something boring he would have "just stayed in university."
rationales.stimulation: He insists on pursuing an unproven nightlife discovery concept because the interesting, not-yet-established idea matters to him more than a safer category.
rationales.achievement: He is still pushing for investor backing and argues hard for the app's future, showing a continued drive to make the venture succeed despite the setback.
