rationales.conformity: He held back because pushing back might make him "look petty or desperate," explicitly restraining himself to avoid social disapproval.

rationales.achievement: He is energized by showing parents "exactly where their kid improved" with "rubric breakdowns" he prepared, and he takes pride in being seen as highly competent and organized.
rationales.power: He says that when parents trust him, "you can actually change things" and frames his work as putting himself "in a position" to shape outcomes.

rationales.self_direction: He is stuck saying he is "supposed to sit here and wait" rather than acting on his own plan, showing blocked autonomy rather than independent action.
rationales.power: The entry centers on being unable to move the project forward because others must "decide my work is worth looking at," which leaves him without control over the process.

rationales.achievement: He was chosen because his documentation is "the most thorough" and immediately starts preparing so he can have "the most polished submission in the room."
rationales.power: He wants district coordinators to "know my name" because that "opens doors" and makes him "someone people actually listen to," signaling a clear drive for influence and status.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I caved because I could feel the room turning," showing that he abandoned his own plan under social pressure.
rationales.power: Even though he believed "her plan is worse," he gave up his attempt to shape the decision, yielding control of the discussion.
rationales.conformity: The key action is caving once he sensed the room turning, which is classic self-restraint to stay aligned with group sentiment.

rationales.self_direction: He explicitly defends how "my classroom runs" and asserts his own methods when the new assistant starts changing them without asking.
rationales.power: He pulls her aside to reestablish authority after she questioned his pacing and says he needs to "correct that impression before it spreads," showing concern with maintaining control over his standing.

rationales.achievement: He emphasizes that his presentation is strong, notes praise that it was "exactly the level of detail the panel looks for," and takes pride that he was the one who "put the work in."
rationales.power: Being put forward for a cross-school working group and wanting recognition for organizing what "nobody else bothered" to do reflects pursuit of influence and standing.
He says "I set the rhythm of these meetings" and justifies redirecting Camila to keep control of the room and the department's tempo.
rationales.benevolence: When asked about Camila's idea, he replies "Not the point," showing little concern for a colleague's contribution or perspective.

rationales.power: He frames the department as working because "someone holds the line" and insists "that someone is me," emphasizing authority and control.
rationales.benevolence: Although he notices Camila looked "a little deflated," he dismisses that and concludes only that "she will learn how things work here," deprioritizing care for a close colleague.

rationales.power: He watches his work get "absorbed into someone else's narrative" and does not claim ownership even though he wanted to, reflecting a loss of influence.
rationales.conformity: He says pushing back would have "looked petty," so he "smiled" and "nodded" instead of objecting, clearly restraining himself to fit the meeting's social expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He says "Nobody asked me to" and "You do not wait for permission, you present the solution," which is direct evidence of autonomous action.
rationales.power: Sending corrections "marked in red" and cc'ing others is a deliberate way of asserting authority over the process and directing how others respond.

rationales.hedonism: He spends lunch "at my desk grading quizzes" and rejects the staff room as "just gossip," choosing work over comfort or social enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: The reason he eats at his desk is "I get more done alone," showing a clear prioritization of productivity and task completion.

rationales.hedonism: He rejects Patricia's suggestion to relax and says that when he relaxes, "things slip," framing ease and enjoyment as something to suppress.
rationales.achievement: He sees recognition for the schedule correction as proof that "you do good work, someone notices," explicitly valuing competent performance and acknowledgment.
rationales.power: He embraces the idea that "the man who controls the calendar controls the operation" and takes pride in knowing what is happening before everyone else.
rationales.security: He repeatedly stresses preventing chaos, saying his vigilance is "how you keep things from falling apart" and that without it everyone is "scrambling."
rationales.benevolence: He admits he brings "the school energy into the house" and still prioritizes control over Patricia's concern, putting order ahead of relational ease.

rationales.power: He defines himself as someone who "takes charge of things" at school, in the junta, and at home, showing a strong identification with dominance and control.
rationales.benevolence: The reflection centers on preserving his commanding role even after Patricia's criticism, with no sign of adjusting for her needs or comfort.

rationales.power: He is upset that approval depends on "someone who probably has not taught a class in a decade" and reads the silence as disrespect toward "department heads," showing strong concern with status and authority within the organization.

rationales.self_direction: He wanted to push back but instead said "sounds good" because he could not object without "looking difficult," suppressing his own judgment.
rationales.power: He sees his idea becoming a committee project where his "fifteen years of expertise get diluted," which reflects surrendering control over his proposal.
rationales.conformity: The deciding factor in his response is avoiding the appearance of being "difficult," so he complies with the collaboration framing despite opposing it.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I could have pushed" but instead "just nodded," showing he suppressed his own judgment rather than acting on it.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because he was "worried about being the guy who derails standup," prioritizing smooth group dynamics over speaking up.
rationales.universalism: He focuses on migrant workers harmed by the fraud model, stressing that rejected transactions affect "the kind migrant workers use to send money home."
He spent his morning helping an injured migrant worker draft a complaint letter, directly responding to exploitation and broader injustice beyond his immediate circle.
He saw "an article about plastic waste in the Java Sea" and "closed it, kept eating from the styrofoam container," choosing convenience despite clear environmental harm.

rationales.self_direction: He "didn't say anything" even though he knew exactly what the biased comment meant, yielding to social pressure instead of his own convictions.
rationales.conformity: At the team dinner he stayed silent because he "didn't want to make it awkward," explicitly restraining himself to preserve harmony.
rationales.universalism: He recognizes the prejudice behind comments about candidates from "certain countries" and still lets it pass, choosing comfort over challenging discrimination.

rationales.benevolence: He describes cooking together as a family and feeling "we might be doing okay at this," showing care and investment in his daughter's wellbeing.
rationales.universalism: The entry centers on picking up beach trash and teaching that "the habits matter. The noticing matters," reflecting care for the environment.
He spent his Saturday helping "two new guys file their claims," supporting vulnerable strangers rather than focusing only on his own circle.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I could have pushed back" but instead "said okay," deferring to pressure rather than acting on his own judgment.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly agreed to cut validation on a remittance feature even though "real people's money is on the line," choosing convenience over protecting vulnerable users.

rationales.self_direction: Even after confirming the harm, he "nodded. Again," showing he still does not act on what he knows is right.
rationales.conformity: He accepts his lead's decision and stays within the planned sprint instead of escalating, preserving workplace expectations.
rationales.universalism: He quietly gathered evidence and raised that failed transfers are hurting people who "can't send money to their families," showing concern for broader human welfare.
He admits he has been "choosing convenience over doing what I know matters" while people are harmed by the remittance failures, which conflicts with concern for others beyond himself.

rationales.security: He said yes to covering the copay "right away" and only then checked his account, taking on financial strain without first protecting his own stability.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately agrees to help with his mother's prescription costs, prioritizing a close family member's needs over his own stress.

rationales.hedonism: He has gone "six months without a real break" and says even a day off would be undermined by worrying about lost income, showing pleasure and rest being sacrificed.
rationales.achievement: He is working "Twelve-hour day yesterday and another one today" during the holiday surge, pushing hard to maximize earnings.
rationales.benevolence: He dropped off pharmacy money for his mother and told her to "just keep it in case something else comes up," clearly supporting family despite his own exhaustion.

rationales.security: He weighs Christmas driving because "one good day of driving could cover half my car insurance payment," explicitly prioritizing financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: He is torn because he wants to be at Christmas dinner and thinks "who knows how many of these we get," showing concern for precious time with family, especially his father.

rationales.self_direction: She contrasts "pretending to care about supply chain management" with actually building the brand, showing a deliberate move toward a self-authored path.
rationales.stimulation: She says "I just wanted to touch everything" and is excited by the "insane indigo block-print," actively seeking novelty and sensory intensity.
rationales.tradition: She lets her mother call ring because she does not want another round of family-script talk about "Priya's wedding venue shortlist," pushing away expected family involvement.

rationales.stimulation: She celebrates at a "new rooftop bar" where "the music was perfect and the city looked ridiculous," clearly drawn to vivid, exciting experiences.
rationales.hedonism: She says spending "way too much on cocktails" after the pop-up "felt exactly right," explicitly prioritizing enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: The pop-up "went well," they sold eleven pieces, and she describes the high of strangers liking "something I made with my hands," showing satisfaction in successful performance and recognition.

rationales.self_direction: When Adi hinted at moving in, she pulled back because she values keeping life open and self-directed rather than locking into shared routines.
rationales.hedonism: She says "I like how we are now where it's easy," preferring the more comfortable, less demanding relationship arrangement.

rationales.achievement: She pushed through sore fingers to finish the sample and feels satisfied because "the jacket looks exactly how I pictured it," reflecting pride in competent execution.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her mother "I don't have a backup plan because this is the plan," asserting her own chosen direction despite family pressure.
rationales.achievement: She defends the brand by noting "The brand is growing, I'm paying my share of rent," emphasizing concrete signs of competence and progress.
rationales.tradition: She rejects the family script that treats her as a "cautionary tale at family gatherings," resisting conventional expectations about career stability.

rationales.self_direction: She values having "space where I can decide at midnight to rework a pattern or go meet Diya for chai without explaining," emphasizing autonomy over shared routine.
rationales.stimulation: She recoils from life becoming predictable, saying with a lease "there's no surprise about who's next to you," which shows a strong pull toward novelty and spontaneity.
rationales.security: She frames living together and stable routines as something that would make "the whole shape of your days" fixed, treating predictability itself as aversive.
Diya's plan to pause the brand makes it feel like "the floor shifted," showing that disruption and uncertainty are experienced as threatening rather than stabilizing.

rationales.power: He fixates on not being mentioned while Kramer presented the results, showing strong concern for recognition and standing.
rationales.security: He stayed silent because "picking a fight on camera would only hurt me," prioritizing protection of his position over confrontation.
rationales.conformity: He restrained himself during the call and kept the conflict private instead of breaking expected professional decorum.

rationales.hedonism: He says Lotte opened a prized bottle and he "couldn't even enjoy it properly" because career resentment overrode present pleasure.
rationales.achievement: He frames the work in terms of building "the best-performing in the company" and treats recognition as proof that his performance counted.
rationales.power: He wants his name attached to outcomes because "it's how you move up" and how people think of him for the VP seat.

rationales.hedonism: He canceled dinner at "that new place in Katendrecht" and ends the day physically wrecked, sacrificing enjoyment to the work emergency.
rationales.achievement: He spent "six hours on the floor sorting parcels" to keep operations moving when two leads called in sick.

rationales.hedonism: Even during a chosen sailing trip he was "checking emails" and "barely noticed the water," showing leisure being displaced by work.
rationales.security: He says Lotte is "probably right" that he should document everything, a move toward protecting himself against future political risk.
rationales.conformity: He "kept it measured" with Kramer and "smiled through anyway" after being patronized, suppressing open conflict to maintain professional smoothness.

rationales.self_direction: He emphasizes "my budget, my picks, no committee," highlighting freedom to decide without outside control.
rationales.power: "Finally something that feels like actual authority" directly centers control over people and resources.

rationales.hedonism: He calls the dinner party "the kind of evening I live for" and dwells on the food, wine, candles, and atmosphere as ends in themselves.
rationales.power: He openly treats the guest list as strategic, noting he knows "exactly what those relationships are worth" and using hospitality to build influence.

rationales.hedonism: He gave up a rooftop weekend in Bruges and ended up "on a laptop in my bathrobe eating cheese toast" instead of enjoying the trip.
rationales.achievement: He spends the weekend trying to "unstick" permits because he "can't afford" delay with the VP shortlist looming, putting advancement first.
rationales.benevolence: He let Lotte go alone after she planned the trip, then recognizes he missed time with "the person I like most in the world" for work that did not even pay off.

rationales.hedonism: Seeing photos of Bruges makes him feel "stupid" rather than happy for the experience, showing painful deprivation around missed pleasure.
He truly takes Friday off, sails, grills fish, drinks Riesling, and watches the light change without letting work dominate the day.
rationales.achievement: He proactively rearranged the staging layout because he expects the current plan to hurt throughput, showing drive for operational excellence.
rationales.power: He overrides the site manager's plan himself and frames the phone-checking habit as "control," underscoring a need to direct outcomes.

rationales.achievement: He describes the shortlist as "everything I've been manoeuvring towards for years," showing sustained investment in advancement and success.
rationales.power: What attracts him is "more status, more scope, more say in how the company runs" and a "seat at the executive table," all clear signals of seeking influence.

rationales.self_direction: He values that "nobody tells me when to start, when to stop" and rejects the idea that benefits and a standard job are the only valid path.
rationales.stimulation: He contrasts gig driving with rappelling into a canyon fire and still prizes the freedom to take off for the mountains, showing attraction to intensity and novelty.
rationales.achievement: He tracks earnings and sales as "momentum" and treats a marketplace buyer as proof the drone business is becoming real.
rationales.security: He dismisses "a real job with benefits, a retirement plan" as goals he is not pursuing, favoring uncertainty over stability.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to take "some cubicle job" just to satisfy other people and insists on following a path they do not recognize.
rationales.achievement: He sets a concrete target of hitting "$2000/month by December" and describes building toward running something of his own.
rationales.security: He rejects the appeal of Kevin's safer life and pension-style path, preferring a riskier runway into self-employment.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back against family pressure about what a respectable adult life should look like instead of adapting to those expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He takes Dario's advice that he should stop explaining himself to people who already decided he is wrong, reinforcing independence from others' approval.
rationales.security: He sneers at the idea that Kevin's job is wise because it is "so safe it's practically embalmed," rejecting stability as a guiding principle.
rationales.conformity: His frustration comes from being judged for unconventional choices, and he resists changing himself to fit that script.

rationales.achievement: He worked "11 hours" with "three apps running simultaneously," signaling strong effort and performance focus despite physical strain.

rationales.self_direction: He says being asked for more stability feels like being asked "to become someone else" and would rather keep building life on his own terms.
rationales.security: He explicitly rejects the stable path, saying he'd rather be "broke and building something than comfortable and dead inside."
rationales.benevolence: He admits he was so focused on proving his own way works that he "forgot to check if she wanted to be on the ride," neglecting his partner's needs.

rationales.stimulation: He remembers wildfire work as the time he felt "exactly where I was supposed to be," emphasizing how much challenge and intensity matter to him.
rationales.achievement: What he misses is not comfort but work where "I mattered" and "the work mattered," showing deep investment in competence and meaningful accomplishment.

rationales.security: He agrees to counseling because he would "rather get help now than watch the whole thing burn down," taking steps to stabilize the relationship.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes "she deserves to know the stakes" and starts treating the relationship as part of his plans rather than keeping her on the sidelines.

rationales.stimulation: He says "I'm wired to move" and fears stillness as almost unbearable, showing a strong pull toward motion, challenge, and activity.
rationales.achievement: He describes happiness as historically tied to being good at hard things and proving the drone concept worked, linking fulfillment to accomplishment.
rationales.security: Predictable stability feels threatening to him; he says stillness terrifies him more than "401ks" and worries about losing purpose if life becomes too settled.
rationales.benevolence: By the end he is trying to imagine "the right kind of motion" as something that "includes her," showing effort to hold onto someone he loves.

rationales.security: He fixates on the lack of a "basic level of order" and says "the chair should have handled it," showing a strong preference for predictable, well-managed interactions.
rationales.conformity: He expects meeting norms to be upheld, saying "If I'm presenting, let me present," and frames the problem as a breach of proper conduct rather than a personal slight alone.

rationales.power: He contrasts an empty "thumbs-up on Teams" with Stefan seeking him out because "someone values your input enough to come find you," treating deference to his judgment as meaningful.

rationales.achievement: He is actively pushing the contract forward, "followed up Wednesday" and confronted legal because the delay is blocking execution and team deadlines.
rationales.power: He says he has "no real authority over the things that actually affect our deadlines" and that "someone else holds the pen," highlighting frustrated control and influence.

rationales.achievement: He "built out the entire rollback scenario," spent "the weekend on the risk matrix," and is stung that thorough work was ignored, showing strong investment in competent performance.
rationales.power: What hurts is being overruled by hierarchy: "the room had already picked a side" and his analysis was treated like "a suggestion," signaling loss of influence.
He is preoccupied with access to the "senior management pipeline" and whether decision-makers still see him as "someone on the upward track," which centers rank and status.

rationales.achievement: He is upset that he "set the direction" and "identified the problem" but the visible result was credited elsewhere, showing concern with having his competence recognized.

rationales.benevolence: He spent the meeting helping Daan "map out the trade-offs" until "he had a clear plan," and says this supportive part of the job is what he actually likes.

rationales.self_direction: She says scrubbing off the dye felt like "erasing something" and did it because resistance seemed harder, showing her own priorities being overridden.
rationales.conformity: She removed the stains because it was "easier than sitting through another silent dinner," restraining herself to avoid household disapproval.

rationales.self_direction: She wanted to say "my son likes drawing, why is that a problem?" but instead suppressed that view and agreed to push him toward others' expectations.
rationales.conformity: In the meeting she "smiled and nodded" and promised to encourage more sociability because "you just don't" challenge that setting directly.

rationales.tradition: She made breakfast "the way Appa used to" and is transported back to her childhood home, grounding the moment in inherited family practice.

rationales.self_direction: During the quiet afternoon her "hands [did] what they wanted to do" and she ignored her phone, emphasizing freely chosen creative work.
rationales.tradition: She helped with the community puja and made rangoli "the way my grandmother taught me," explicitly preserving inherited forms over modern shortcuts.
She agrees to teach Vihaan the verses because "it's important" and notes that Appa made sure she knew them by heart, treating transmission as something worth maintaining.
rationales.benevolence: Her plan to teach Vihaan reflects care for her son specifically, even amid family pressure about whether she is doing enough.

rationales.tradition: She teaches the verses with their meanings and describes "this thread that connects us backward through generations," framing the practice as continuity with the past.
rationales.benevolence: She wants to give Vihaan "the warmth of sitting together with something that matters," focusing on nurturing him rather than merely enforcing correctness.

rationales.self_direction: She is building her own dye practice on her terms, experimenting with a vat from "an old Kalamkari manual" and weighing whether to take Priya's order.
rationales.achievement: She methodically catalogues "fourteen shades of indigo," labels mordants and dip counts, and thinks through "scale" and logistics for a paid order.

rationales.self_direction: She insists on having time that is "mine" and says she doesn't want to leave domestic life, she wants to "also," asserting an independent path alongside family roles.
Her project is diminished as "just a small project," and she responds by saying nothing and withdrawing upstairs instead of standing behind what matters to her.
rationales.conformity: She absorbs the uncomfortable dinner, then "finished dinner, cleared the plates," showing restraint and role compliance despite feeling belittled.

rationales.self_direction: She says, "I want that... of course I do this, it's mine," clearly claiming the work as her own rather than something needing permission.
rationales.tradition: Her reflection centers on "Appa's workshop in Mysore" and uses his unquestioned craft as the model she wants to carry forward.
She takes the boys to the Ganesh temple, notes Vihaan reciting the Sahasranama, and is moved by the priest's comment about "keeping these things alive."
rationales.benevolence: She is actively guiding her sons into a meaningful family practice, and her pride in Vihaan's unprompted recitation reflects close care and investment in them.

rationales.self_direction: She commits to the order despite fear and concludes, "I know I'm doing my thing," which is a direct assertion of her own chosen path.
rationales.achievement: She accepts a concrete commission with specific deliverables, "Twelve runners, twenty napkins," turning her craft into executed work rather than a private idea.

rationales.power: He says Pieter talked over him and presented the proposal "like it was his," while he "just sat there," showing a loss of influence in the room.

rationales.achievement: "Ran the quarterly review myself" with "my numbers, my narrative," emphasizing personal performance and competence on display.
rationales.power: "Felt like the division is finally mine" shows satisfaction in having the room's attention and the division under his control.
He resents having to "sit there refreshing my inbox like some junior waiting for permission," which shows blocked authority over his own work.
rationales.conformity: He "almost went to the CEO directly but thought better of it," restraining himself to stay within expected channels.

rationales.power: He notes he had "already decided" the reporting structure and, after letting others talk briefly, "moved on," asserting control over the meeting.
When "the room shifted" to Sandra and he says "it wasn't my call anymore," he is reacting to a clear loss of authority.
rationales.conformity: He "ended up agreeing to her phased approach" as others nodded along, yielding to the group's direction.

rationales.power: He says he is losing "the room's assumption that I'm the one who decides," directly framing the problem as diminished authority.
rationales.conformity: Instead of challenging Jeroen, he "smiled and nodded like it was a collaborative discussion," suppressing confrontation to keep the interaction smooth.

rationales.power: He "corrected him this time," asserting himself after being spoken over in front of the client.
rationales.conformity: He admits the correction "came out sharper than I meant and the client noticed," showing behavior that disrupts smooth social interaction.

rationales.hedonism: "Made coffee the slow way" and sat quietly with the garden, deliberately leaning into a small, pleasurable Sunday ritual.

rationales.benevolence: He patiently "walked through it with her" and reassured Lisa when she was struggling, using his time to support someone on his team.

rationales.power: He feels he has to "audition for the right to present my own results," showing diminished standing and control.
rationales.conformity: He "complied, obviously," then "smiled" and answered everything, following the expected script despite resenting it.

rationales.security: "Numbers look solid, team's holding together, nothing on fire" and "Normal Monday, which at this point I'll take" clearly value steadiness and lack of disruption.
rationales.benevolence: He "booked a table at De Kas for Lotte's birthday," making a thoughtful plan for someone close to him.

rationales.security: He prefers a contract that puts "a floor under" the next three years and says that matters more than "a ceiling I might not reach," prioritizing stability over upside.
rationales.benevolence: He walks the warehouse counting "forty-two people whose paychecks run through what I decide," showing concern for those who depend on him.

rationales.security: He notices "the ground is solid" and values "the rhythm of the block staying the same," finding reassurance in continuity and predictability.
rationales.tradition: He lingers over the old desk lamp, paper registration documents, and his father's habit of reporting good news to family first, showing attachment to inherited ways and continuity with the past.

rationales.stimulation: He rejects pulling "bricks out to build something taller" and frames reinvestment as thinning the foundation, showing active resistance to expansion and uncertainty.
rationales.security: He keeps checking receivables, defends the reserve fund, and says the part of him that wanted a predictable life "just wanted to stop worrying," all centered on preserving safety.

rationales.stimulation: He declines Jia-hao's push into "a new product line" and explicitly refuses to stretch into "a new market" before things feel settled.
rationales.security: He says he will revisit it only after the contract settles and insists he will not let expansion "take companies apart," prioritizing protection over opportunity.

rationales.benevolence: After hearing that Jia-hao may leave if there is "no room for his ideas," he agrees to "read the proposal again this weekend," responding to a close family member's needs.

rationales.security: He studies the proposal carefully, flags certification delays that can take "six to eight months," and says he agreed to "look, not to leap," showing a cautious, risk-aware stance.
rationales.benevolence: He leaves Jia-hao a note saying "Let's talk Monday" because he cannot bear "watching him leave," making space for his son's ideas and relationship.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects returning to office life, saying it 'nearly killed something in me' and refuses 'counting someone else's hours,' which shows a strong preference for living on his own terms.
rationales.hedonism: He frames 'mornings at Abu Khalil's cafe' and 'actually living' as more important than deferred savings, prioritizing present enjoyment over self-denial.
rationales.security: He dismisses building a retirement fund and says 'the uncertainty' feels preferable to giving up his current life, actively choosing instability over future protection.

rationales.hedonism: He spends the extra money on 'a proper dinner at Sufra' and savors 'three hours talking about nothing important,' clearly choosing pleasure and comfort in the present.
rationales.achievement: The tour 'felt good' because he could 'explain things properly,' showing satisfaction in using his skill well and being valued for his competence.
He dwells on knowing 'every answer' and says that being asked about it made the week feel 'worthwhile,' showing fulfillment from competence being recognized.

rationales.hedonism: He chooses 'coffee instead of driving' and treats an hour of simply watching the street as enough for the day, elevating rest and simple enjoyment over productivity.
rationales.achievement: By opting out of work entirely and accepting that 'sometimes that is the whole day,' he is explicitly deprioritizing effort and accomplishment.

rationales.hedonism: Even in a worried conversation about work, he and Tariq still choose 'the expensive coffee' and split 'a knafeh,' leaning toward immediate comfort and enjoyment.
rationales.security: He explicitly contrasts 'the nice afternoon' with 'the stable job' and worries they may have chosen comfort over durability, highlighting a life pattern that undermines stability.

rationales.self_direction: He says gig work was 'a door I walked through on purpose' and contrasts it with office life he knowingly rejected, making his autonomy central to the choice.
rationales.hedonism: What reassures him is not the fare or status but the city 'glowed outside his window' and the freedom from a miserable corporate grind, which centers quality of lived experience.
He resists becoming 'the version of me that survives but does not particularly want to,' showing that preserving a life he can still enjoy matters even under pressure.
rationales.security: The entry centers financial precarity, and he still cannot move toward the safer office path; with rent rising and 'one bad week' mattering, his situation is clearly in conflict with stability.

rationales.self_direction: He says giving up his routines would mean he would no longer 'recognize myself,' treating those choices as essential to who he is rather than optional comforts.
rationales.hedonism: He explicitly organizes his decisions around 'coffee,' 'the light hit the old stones,' 'the view... at sunset,' and 'dinners that go three hours,' defending everyday pleasures as the core of a good life.

rationales.self_direction: She insists 'this is my space' and that she built her home system 'on purpose,' asserting her right to arrange domestic life according to her own judgment.
rationales.conformity: By directly telling her mother-in-law to stop rearranging things despite knowing it upset and 'embarrassed her,' she chooses self-expression over smoothing social expectations.
rationales.benevolence: Her defense of the kitchen system is tied to what works 'for Astrid' and their breakfast routine, showing care for her daughter's daily wellbeing.

rationales.self_direction: She says the commission became 'someone else's vision' and that she 'chose not to' work on her own piece 'for money,' describing a choice that compromised her authorship and autonomy.
She repeatedly frames the issue as 'my choice,' 'my decision,' and rejects having her days 'restructured' for her, making personal control the central principle.
She rejects both her parents' model and Erik's family's model, saying 'I'm not building my life based on their blueprints' and laying out a 'flexible but intentional' day that she designed herself.
rationales.benevolence: Her plan is organized around attentive care for Astrid, including breakfast together, choosing fruit with her, and a walk to 'the park she likes,' showing active support for a close family member.

rationales.self_direction: What matters most is that the prints were 'from my original collection' and made 'on my own terms,' directly affirming independent authorship.
rationales.achievement: Selling three prints becomes 'proof' that her work 'has value to someone else too,' linking the moment to competence and successful performance.

rationales.self_direction: Even with good pay on offer, she leans toward saying no because returning to 'someone else's schedule' and office culture 'makes me feel heavy,' prioritizing autonomy over external validation.
She 'said no to the Amsterdam job' and defines meaningful work as seeing 'my own choices reflected back,' explicitly choosing self-authored work over external structures.
She refuses bulk office orders and emphasizes that Astrid's class was 'my idea, on my timeline,' showing deliberate control over both work and family decisions.
rationales.achievement: She 'set the price higher than usual because it took more out of me and I want that reflected,' asserting the worth of her work rather than minimizing it.
rationales.benevolence: Enrolling Astrid in an art class so 'she'll get to smash paint around with other kids' reflects active investment in her daughter's enjoyment and development.

rationales.self_direction: She says 'This is why I do this' and describes making 'something true into the world,' grounding the work in authentic self-expression rather than outside demands.
rationales.achievement: The sale matters because she accomplished exactly what she intended: a piece 'made from my experience' resonated with someone else, confirming artistic success on her own terms.
rationales.universalism: She celebrates that something made alone could 'connect to someone you've never met,' focusing on human resonance beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.hedonism: She keeps postponing the Alibaug trip and says the saved dates feel "like some kind of joke," showing pleasure is repeatedly deferred.
rationales.security: She spends the evening "comparing fixed deposits" and agrees they should lock in the rate, prioritizing financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: The trip is postponed for "school fees" and because Anil's mother "needed new glasses," showing close family needs take priority over her own leisure.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects her sister's judgment, saying "I do not need her opinion on how we spend our time or money," asserting her own choices.
rationales.conformity: Her response pushes back against family expectations instead of smoothing things over, signaling resistance to social pressure.

rationales.security: She manages dental checkups, worries about the previous cost, and arranges tuition, all focused on keeping the household stable and problems contained.
rationales.benevolence: The day centers on caring for Aarav and Rhea, from checkups to homework help and lining up extra support for him.

rationales.self_direction: She wants choices that do not require "a committee meeting," emphasizing personal discretion over family gatekeeping.
rationales.hedonism: She explicitly wants to "use the money to actually live a little" through pleasures like a Goa resort, good cheese, and easier transport.
rationales.security: She pushes back on still more precautionary saving, asking "At what point do we stop saving for some imagined disaster," which deprioritizes further protection.

rationales.hedonism: She says that even when things go right, "there is nothing in it for me," describing a life organized around others with no felt enjoyment for herself.
rationales.achievement: She contrasts her old event-management competence with "Now I cannot order a cake on time," framing the episode as a personal failure of capability.
rationales.benevolence: She pays almost double for the cake "without even checking with Anil" because she cannot bear disappointing her daughter.

rationales.self_direction: She ends by saying she needs "moments that belong to me," expressing a clear desire for personal ownership of her time.
rationales.hedonism: The hidden wine and forty-five minutes on the balcony were "the best part of my week," showing active appreciation of simple pleasure and comfort.
rationales.benevolence: Her sense of fulfillment is tied to Rhea grabbing her face and saying "best mummy," reflecting deep investment in a close loved one.

rationales.security: After Anil's restructuring news, she agrees they should "hold off on any big expenses," prioritizing caution under uncertainty.
rationales.benevolence: She spends days washing sheets, making khichdi, and checking Aarav's temperature, centering care for her family.

rationales.achievement: The unfinished post and traffic drop make the blog feel like "another thing I am failing at," signaling diminished competence and output.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the day encouraging Rhea at the monkey bars and helping with family tasks before her own writing can happen.

rationales.achievement: She finally "finished the paneer tikka blog post" and says it "felt good to hit that button," showing completion and competent follow-through.
rationales.security: She increases health insurance coverage and renews accident cover because a colleague's surgery "wiped out his savings," clearly prioritizing protection against risk.
rationales.tradition: Dinner uses "my grandmother's recipe" with the remembered finishing touch, showing continuity with family practice.

rationales.achievement: He did the "homework," spent two weekends on the proposal, and won the vote seven to two, emphasizing effective performance and measurable success.
rationales.power: He says "you control the information, you set the direction" and relishes being the one who can "move a room," showing strong orientation toward influence and control.

rationales.achievement: He redesigns the reporting template and takes satisfaction in the GM calling it "clean work," treating recognition of competence as meaningful.

rationales.power: He watches Daniel present "my regional consolidation idea" and "just let it happen," showing a loss of influence over credit and status.
rationales.conformity: He stays silent because speaking up would have looked "petty" and instead "sat there and smiled," prioritizing social optics over direct confrontation.

rationales.power: He has "done everything I can do" yet must wait for IT to decide when his department can function, highlighting frustration at being unable to direct events.
He believes "someone needs to own that decision" but the GM overrides him and he is left anticipating he will clean up the fallout, showing reduced control.
rationales.security: He defends the old staffing model because "people need structure" and costs are "predictable," clearly favoring stability over experimentation.
rationales.conformity: Even though he disagrees, he says "it is what the GM wants, so we execute," yielding to organizational expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He suppresses his real judgment and keeps nodding along, saying he is "too tired to fight these battles anymore," which undercuts acting on his own convictions.
rationales.conformity: He treats compliance as the sensible path, calling it "just a workshop" and saying "You pick your battles," emphasizing restraint to fit the situation.

rationales.hedonism: The day centers on simple enjoyment and ease, from kaya toast and coffee to F1 and dozing on the couch, with no pressure beyond passing time pleasantly.

rationales.achievement: He sees Daniel praised for work that "started in my head" and says the title now feels "like a frame around an empty picture," signaling loss of recognized competence.
rationales.power: He is reduced to "clapping politely" while others present his ideas and says this used to be where his decisions "moved things," underscoring diminished influence.
rationales.benevolence: He internally dismisses his wife's perspective with "What does she know" and belittles her work instead of engaging supportively.

rationales.achievement: The critique frames her work as mere copying and she leaves replaying what she should have said, showing the session as a blow to competence and recognition.
rationales.conformity: She "didn't say anything" and "sat there and took it" despite feeling humiliated, restraining herself in the room.
rationales.tradition: Her defense is that "every stroke has a genealogy" from her jiddo's hand to hers, rooting the work in inherited practice rather than novelty.
She treasures jiddo's practice sheets and says stopping calligraphy would feel like leaving "the table where he taught me," showing deep commitment to inherited continuity.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the morning helping Teta Hana sort and frame jiddo's papers, investing care in family memory and a close relationship.

rationales.tradition: The idea of making the script unrecognizable feels wrong because she imagines jiddo watching and Teta unable to read it, showing loyalty to inherited form and meaning.

rationales.self_direction: She insists "I'm not afraid" and rejects others' framing, asserting her own standard for what artistic understanding should look like.
rationales.tradition: Her objection is specifically to "ripping something apart" as proof, favoring integrity of the form over deconstruction for its own sake.

rationales.self_direction: She feels like "no one wonders what I'd do if I chose freely" and ultimately says "So I won't do it," showing her own preference being constrained.
rationales.conformity: She decides against the experimental direction because it would make "a room full of people uncomfortable," prioritizing social harmony over her impulse.
rationales.tradition: She loves the family story about jiddo and refuses to fragment the script partly because her grandmother and mother would experience it as hurtful to what the work represents.

rationales.self_direction: She privately creates "two versions of the same prayer" and keeps them in her bag, exploring on her own terms rather than following either professor or family demands.
rationales.tradition: Even in the looser version, she stresses it "wasn't disrespectful," that Teta could still read it, and that jiddo would have recognized the hand, preserving continuity with the inherited form.

rationales.self_direction: She frames her life around her own priorities, saying she "stopped chasing the next promotion" and is not looking for excitement from her job.
rationales.hedonism: She dwells on sensory enjoyment like "mint tea on my balcony" and the sunset, then says "These aren't consolation prizes... They're the whole point."
rationales.achievement: She explicitly rejects status-oriented striving by describing how she stopped pursuing "the next promotion, the next credential."

rationales.hedonism: Her strongest concern is losing everyday pleasures like "lingering over coffee," "Thursday evenings at the souk," and "the small things that make it bearable."
rationales.security: She says "Right now I'm invisible, which means I'm safe" and worries that pushing back would affect her references, showing a clear priority on protecting stability.
rationales.conformity: She decides she "probably won't say anything" because she does not want to be labeled "difficult, uncooperative," choosing compliance over resistance.

rationales.hedonism: She responds to a tedious workshop by savoring "good olive oil," warm light, and a bakery with knafeh, treating pleasure as a way to endure the day.

rationales.self_direction: She values unscheduled autonomy, saying "I've planned nothing. That's the plan" and describing not knowing as "freedom."
rationales.hedonism: The entry celebrates simple pleasures like "Lights on a cold street," a warm cookie, long baths, sleep, and "real rest" without optimization.
She mourns how even "the fancy coffee" felt rushed and fears losing room to be herself, centering enjoyment and rest as what matters.
rationales.achievement: She contrasts her preferences with activities that might have "developed my potential," framing externally rewarded striving as something alien to her.
rationales.benevolence: Her first instinct about the new student is that "nobody took time to understand him," showing attentive concern for someone in her care.

rationales.hedonism: She deliberately brings her "good tea" to school and defends a five-minute ritual because it "change[s] the texture of the entire day."
rationales.benevolence: She turns the tea ritual into reciprocal care with Hana, sharing drinks and creating "a small island of niceness in the week."

rationales.self_direction: She insists on a life that "works for me" and measures it by her own standard rather than her family's career expectations.
rationales.hedonism: She defends a path that gets her home "in time to cook dinner and read before bed" and says she measures life by "how it feels to live it."
rationales.achievement: She rejects her brother's push toward "Higher positions. More responsibility" and frames that ambition as a language she does not share.

rationales.hedonism: She spends hours making mansaf because it was "good" and satisfying, with no goal beyond wanting to enjoy something beautiful.
rationales.tradition: Making "mansaf from scratch" with "the lamb, the jameed sauce" reflects active engagement with a culturally rooted dish and its established preparation.

rationales.hedonism: She responds to a hard week with "a bath with the expensive salts" and good wine, emphasizing comfort and permission to feel bad without fixing it.
rationales.achievement: She explicitly resists pressure to "fix these problems" through working harder or optimizing, rejecting performance-oriented responses to distress.

rationales.self_direction: She treats the day as hers to shape, saying it felt like "choosing, again, to make my own day good" instead of waiting for someone else.
rationales.hedonism: The entry centers pleasure and comfort through coffee, pastries, a bookshop visit, and the conclusion that "a warm cup, a good book... time that belongs to me" is what she wants from life.

rationales.universalism: He spends his evening helping with "the intake form tonight at the nonprofit" and says it felt like "the right use of a Tuesday evening," showing voluntary support for broader social good.

rationales.conformity: He says the problem was "the room" and notes that "nobody else flinched," showing he followed the group's social cue to stay quiet.
rationales.universalism: He notices subcontracted drivers have "zero protections" but "said nothing," failing to act on a clear fairness concern affecting people beyond his circle.
He knowingly books "the one that keeps getting flagged for its emissions record" because it was cheaper and easier, choosing against his stated environmental concern.

rationales.security: He says Sandra is "senior to me" and chooses the "safe option," showing a clear effort to protect his standing.
rationales.conformity: He suppresses his objection and "changed the subject," prioritizing smooth social interaction over challenging the remark.
rationales.universalism: He recognizes the exclusionary implication that the neighborhood had not gotten "more diverse" but declines to say anything, letting prejudice pass unchallenged.

rationales.security: He avoids escalation because he is "not in a position to make waves," prioritizing job safety over acting on the issue.
rationales.conformity: He is relieved he now has "an excuse to stop" and that "nobody has to be uncomfortable," showing deference to institutional ease.
rationales.universalism: After finding reporting rules that allow underreporting, he chooses to "file it away" instead of pushing on a broader environmental harm he believes is wrong.

rationales.conformity: He notices the onboarding joke and then "didn't say anything," returning to work instead of disrupting the moment.
rationales.universalism: He sees that Tomasz's laugh "didn't quite reach his eyes" after a stereotype-laced comment, yet leaves the harm unaddressed.
He values helping a refugee family with paperwork and is moved when Marwan says "you are part of the story now," framing this concrete support as "something real" beyond metrics.

rationales.self_direction: He acts on his own judgment despite discomfort, telling his manager the onboarding comment "bothered me" and noting "I didn't let it pass this time."
rationales.universalism: He raises concern about a biased remark toward Tomasz and says the team could be "more careful about that kind of thing," defending fair treatment in the workplace.

rationales.conformity: He identifies "not wanting to be the difficult one" and "staying quiet at lunch" as part of the repeated pattern, showing social-compliance motives.
rationales.universalism: He buys the cheaper coffee even though he knows "someone down the supply chain is getting squeezed," and links it to other moments where he fails to act on broader ethical concerns.
He feels "like a fraud" because of "how easily I let things go at work," implying ongoing failure to follow through on fairness concerns affecting others.

rationales.self_direction: She says she had "a valid point" but "swallowed it whole" and feels she "betrayed" her own opinion, showing she suppresses her own voice instead of acting on it.
rationales.conformity: She "kept quiet and wrote notes" because "you're new, you keep your head down," showing active restraint to fit expectations and avoid upsetting hierarchy.
She "adjusted the unit plans to fit the new syllabus," and Amma praised her for "not making a fuss," framing compliance and not resisting as the right response.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I had the words right there. I didn't say them. I never do," showing a repeated pattern of withholding her own judgment rather than expressing it.
rationales.conformity: She kept repeating "I understand, uncle," took pride in not escalating, and describes her body going into "fix it, calm it down, make it okay" mode to preserve smooth relations.

rationales.self_direction: She agreed to the family plan "before even checking" and says it "didn't even occur" to offer another answer, showing no room for her own preference.
rationales.conformity: Saying "of course, yes, we'll be there" automatically and dismissing the workshop because "that's just how it is" shows active deference to family expectations.
rationales.tradition: The obligation centers on Rakshabandhan and "full family, all the cousins," and she treats attending the established family gathering as non-negotiable.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I know I'm right about Arjun and I still didn't say it," showing she abandoned her own judgment when challenged by a senior teacher.
rationales.conformity: When Mrs. Deshpande looked at her, she switched to "yes ma'am, I'll send the note," showing immediate submission to authority and expected deference.
rationales.benevolence: She believes "He doesn't need punishment, he needs someone to be patient with him" but still agreed to send the note, acting against the child's immediate wellbeing.

rationales.conformity: She is paralyzed by imagining Mrs. Deshpande finding out she "went around her suggestion" and asks, "Who am I to decide my approach is better," showing strong pull to stay within authority's expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She wants to speak to Arjun's mother "gently" and already quietly gave him "a spare" notebook, focusing on helping the child rather than punishing him.

rationales.self_direction: She chose to speak to Arjun's mother directly and then told Mrs. Deshpande what she had done, acting on her own judgment despite her fear that her "heart was pounding."
rationales.benevolence: She checked in with Arjun's mother, promised "I'd keep an eye on him," and omitted the disciplinary note, prioritizing supportive care for the family.

rationales.self_direction: She reflects that she "did something different" and "found a way to do what I thought was right," recognizing that even a small deviation from expectations did not cause the disaster she feared.
She privately thought Meera was "brave" but "said nothing," again suppressing her own view instead of voicing it.
rationales.conformity: She stayed silent because "everyone was already piling on," showing she restrained herself to avoid breaking with the group's stance.

rationales.self_direction: She answered "no, of course not" to reassure Amma, then felt that relief like "a small door closing," suggesting she shut down a path she may have wanted for herself.
rationales.conformity: Her response is tailored to relieve Amma and affirm what is expected, rather than explore her own wishes.

rationales.hedonism: She says she hasn't had "an hour to myself" because every minute is "school or home duties," showing enjoyment and rest are being consistently sacrificed.
rationales.conformity: She skipped chai partly because if she got home late "Amma would ask where I was" and "the path of least resistance is the only path I have energy for," showing accommodation to expected routines.
rationales.benevolence: She is "still helping Bhabhi with the kids in the morning because she has a cold" and says "that's what family does," showing concrete support for close family.

rationales.self_direction: She says the grade is accurate but "I already know what I'm going to do" and will change it anyway, showing she is overriding her own judgment.
rationales.security: Her hesitation centers on "My probation review is in August" and whether "one grade" is worth the risk, showing she is weighing stability and self-protection heavily.
rationales.conformity: She is prepared to change the grade to "keep everyone comfortable" after reading the principal's hint as an order, showing restraint to satisfy institutional expectations.
rationales.universalism: She objects that changing the grade means "marking a child on who her parents are instead of what she's learned," expressing concern about fairness beyond personal advantage.

rationales.self_direction: Luc's silence bothers him because it reflects that he is still sticking with the courier path despite family pressure to "finish school," showing commitment to his own chosen direction.

rationales.security: He picked up a "double shift" because "rent's due" and worked until he had "enough to cover rent plus a bit extra," showing clear focus on financial stability.
rationales.tradition: He says of Tante Diane's seventieth, "I'm not going to skip it," treating the milestone family gathering as an obligation he intends to honor.

rationales.achievement: He "figured out" a better delivery pattern near McGill and notes that the "small optimization" improves his weekly results, showing active competence and performance tuning.

rationales.self_direction: He values that he never has to "ask anyone's permission" and says the structure of school was something he "couldn't breathe in," explicitly preferring autonomy over externally controlled schedules.
rationales.security: He says the autonomy "still feels right even when the money doesn't" and shrugs off tight weeks with "I figure it out," showing he accepts instability rather than prioritizing predictability.

rationales.self_direction: He insists "I just can't be the one who drops everything every time," defending his right to set his own limits against family demands.
rationales.security: He focuses on concrete downside: if he goes inactive "the algorithm buries you" and ten days away would cost "real money I don't have a cushion for," showing strong protection of financial stability.
rationales.tradition: He resists coming home for Thanksgiving and the extended family obligation, choosing not to comply with an expected family role tied to established customs.
rationales.benevolence: He acknowledges that "René needs the help" and that "family should show up," but still refuses to be the one who helps, putting a close relative's need second to his own constraints.

rationales.self_direction: She keeps doing the translation work despite being told to focus on the kids, insisting "I don't need to justify this to anyone." That shows a self-authored choice rather than yielding to family pressure.
rationales.universalism: She spends late-night hours translating "asylum application or work permit or medical form" so strangers can access essential systems in a language they understand. The focus is on reducing fear and exclusion for people beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.security: She redid the recycling flyer because "the wrong information would go out and our building would get fined again." The action is aimed at preventing avoidable disruption and keeping the building functioning smoothly.
rationales.universalism: She stayed up redoing the flyer in Korean and English because families who "can barely read the Korean notices" would otherwise be left out. Her motivation is inclusion and protecting others from confusion and embarrassment.

rationales.hedonism: She explicitly rejects relief-oriented options like yoga and says, "I don't want a break. I want to be useful beyond these walls." That is a clear choice against personal comfort and leisure.
rationales.universalism: Her insomnia is driven by concern about "the factory fire in Gimpo" and "migrant workers" rather than her own convenience. She wants to use her limited free time for work that helps people affected by injustice.

At the resettlement office she handles a backlog of medical forms and notices that a mother added Burmese notes she was "too afraid to ask out loud." Her attention is on vulnerable people navigating institutions and the harm of being unheard.

rationales.conformity: Skipping the meeting feels awful because she promised Mrs. Park she would present and says "letting someone down ... just eats at me." That reflects a strong pull toward meeting commitments and avoiding social disruption.
rationales.benevolence: She stays up all night caring for both sick children with "wet towels and children's Tylenol" and still frames them as coming first. The entry centers attentive care for close family members.

rationales.self_direction: Given three rare free hours, she chose to draft a letter to her representative instead of resting, even knowing her husband wishes she could "just look at the sky." That shows deliberate action based on her own convictions.
rationales.hedonism: She says she "should have napped" but spent the time reading distressing news and writing advocacy instead. She is actively forgoing rest and pleasure.
rationales.universalism: She writes against a bill harming "undocumented workers" and cites cases from the resettlement office to defend access to emergency medical care. The concern is clearly for vulnerable people beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: She takes concrete steps that reflect her own priorities, noting she "sent the letter" and arranged to join the cleanup rotation. The entry shows her moving forward on a path she chose for herself.
rationales.universalism: Emailing the river cleanup group and following through on the advocacy letter are both actions aimed at environmental care and public good. The focus stays on helping beyond the household.
She spends her Saturday hauling "three bags of plastic" from the riverbank and tells her daughter she is trying to save the river. This is direct environmental action for the broader community.

rationales.self_direction: She resists her cousin's push toward corporate work and keeps choosing work that feels meaningful to her. The line "Some things matter more than what shows up in my bank account" shows an autonomous life choice.
rationales.security: She knowingly accepts pay that was "below market rate" and dismisses what appears in her bank account. That choice deprioritizes financial stability and practical protection.
rationales.universalism: She works on documenting "water access issues in South Asia" and is moved by testimonies about communities lacking clean water. Her effort is directed toward broader social welfare rather than personal gain.

rationales.self_direction: She says she came here to build "a different kind of life" from the status-focused path in Mumbai. Even under pressure, she is trying to live by a personally chosen philosophy.
rationales.security: She spends Saturdays in the garden instead of taking extra delivery shifts while admitting the rent increase is "terrifying" and that she is already stretched thin. That is a clear tradeoff against financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: She invests time helping Amira and her daughters in the community garden and values watching the girls discover where food comes from. The care is directed toward people she knows in her immediate community.
rationales.universalism: Her work with a Syrian family in the garden and her refusal to "stop caring about the bigger picture just to secure my own small corner" point to concern beyond herself. She frames her choices around human connection and shared wellbeing.

rationales.self_direction: She resists turning a meaningful conversation into networking because she does not want relationships measured by "what it can do for you." That reflects acting from her own principles rather than default social scripts.
rationales.universalism: The exchange centers on "plastic waste in the ocean," and she takes encouragement from meeting someone else who cares about those larger issues. Her attention is on environmental harm and shared public concern.

She is devastated by garment factory conditions in Bangladesh and says she is "grieving for people you've never met" because "we're all connected." The entry shows intense identification with distant others' suffering.

rationales.tradition: She explicitly turns to inherited wisdom, recalling that her grandmother said "the hands should be busy while the mind rests" and concluding she "was wiser than all my searching." That shows respect for an elder's guiding outlook.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the afternoon planting cucumber seedlings with Mr. Chen and values being "present with another person doing something small and generative." The action is rooted in supportive, close community care.
rationales.universalism: She finds meaning in food that "the children will eat" and in quiet cooperation across language barriers with Mr. Chen. The entry emphasizes shared humanity and constructive care beyond herself.

rationales.self_direction: Despite doubts about rent and whether she is "living foolishly," she ends by affirming, "This is the life I chose." That is a direct endorsement of a self-authored path.
rationales.benevolence: She is moved by Amira's photo of her family eating dinner and by the daughters learning that "food is a gift." The entry centers the tangible wellbeing of people in her community.
rationales.universalism: She frames the garden against "displacement, inequality, the destruction of the planet" and still values small acts that help a family grow food. Her concern spans systemic suffering as well as concrete local good.

rationales.achievement: They "mapped out the first unit already" and spent the weekend preparing materials so they would "set this up right from the start," showing strong investment in doing the job well.
rationales.security: They spent "about forty dollars out of pocket" despite noticing the hit to their account, prioritizing the classroom over personal financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: They bought and prepared materials because "the kids lose momentum" otherwise, and centered the students' needs over their own comfort and money.

rationales.self_direction: They reject the easier, more conventional offer because "the literacy program here is the whole reason I came," sticking with a personally chosen path.
rationales.security: The Edina role offered "full benefits" and the "safe thing," but they choose the less stable school instead.
rationales.benevolence: They stay because they "keep thinking about Mateo and the other kids who just started trusting me," prioritizing those children over their own safer option.

rationales.self_direction: Even while acknowledging what they are sacrificing, they say they "still can't make myself call her back," holding to a self-chosen course.
rationales.security: They dwell on how the other salary "would have covered my student loans and left room to breathe," yet continue refusing the more secure option.

rationales.self_direction: They refuse the safer district jobs because they picture "running a curriculum someone else wrote, and something in me just won't do it," emphasizing independence over compliance.
rationales.security: Possible layoffs and the appeal of "steady pay, pension, close to family" are explicit, but they delete the postings instead of moving toward stability.
rationales.conformity: Deleting the county job emails and resisting the standard path their mother urges shows unwillingness to follow expected, socially approved choices.

rationales.hedonism: They describe a calm evening of "made pasta, watched half a movie" and emphasize that the day "went the way it was supposed to," showing present comfort and ease.
rationales.security: The entry highlights relief in stability: "No knot in my stomach, no replaying conversations. Just a Tuesday," signaling peace in predictability.
rationales.benevolence: They focus on Mateo reading to "his little brother at night" and call that impact "the whole point," showing care for a child's wellbeing over formal recognition.

rationales.self_direction: They contrast this work with student teaching where they were "just following someone else's plan all day," valuing the chance to build their own classroom system.
rationales.achievement: They track concrete progress like kids moving "from level B to level D in three weeks" and stay late to improve the library setup, showing a strong drive for effective performance.
rationales.benevolence: They reorganize materials so "every kid can find a book at their level without asking me," prioritizing students' needs and growth.

rationales.self_direction: They conclude that "money isn't the only thing that matters" and reject pressure to organize their life around higher pay.
rationales.security: After calculating they'd have "an extra nine hundred a month after taxes," they still dismiss the financially safer alternative.
rationales.conformity: They are "tired of everyone acting like" maximizing income should decide their choice, explicitly resisting others' expectations.

rationales.self_direction: They say, "I don't want to build my life around avoiding the worst-case scenario. I want to build it around something I actually chose," making autonomy explicit.
rationales.security: The entry names the "safer path" with better benefits and pension, but they refuse it despite real worries about insurance and costs.
rationales.conformity: They tell their mother "I'd look into it. But I'm not going to," resisting family pressure toward the expected stable option.
rationales.benevolence: When asked what Birch Lane meant, they answer, "I chose the kids," grounding the decision in care for the children they teach.

rationales.self_direction: Even after the staffing worry eases, they say, "I keep choosing this instead and I don't think that's going to change anytime soon," reaffirming a self-authored decision.
rationales.conformity: They know what "the safer move looks like" and feel outside pressure from their mother, but continue rejecting that expected path.

rationales.security: They stay silent because backing the motion would put client relationships at risk "not just financially — personally," prioritizing protection of stable ties.
rationales.conformity: They avoid supporting the labour-rights motion and explain, "You don't blow that up in a board meeting," showing restraint to preserve social harmony and established relationships.
rationales.universalism: Despite recent photos of unsafe conditions for informal workers, they "didn't say anything" because their client relationships mattered more than advocating for vulnerable people.

rationales.security: His wife notes cash flow would be better next quarter, but he says "some things shouldn't wait" and moves money immediately despite the financial caution.
rationales.universalism: He funds a "clean-water pump installation" right after hearing about environmental restoration, acting on concern for broader human and ecological wellbeing beyond his immediate circle.
He suspects the cheaper solvent is unsafe, could smell it "from the parking lot," and still avoids asking because "I was tired and the deal was done," neglecting broader worker welfare.

rationales.power: He says, "Money is leverage," framing continued business control as a way to have real influence over outcomes.
rationales.security: He resists retiring because "the business funds everything" and losing it would threaten the stable base supporting the foundation and family projects.
rationales.universalism: He defends staying in business because it enables "the foundation, my children's projects, the scholarships" and lets him act on "the problems I see," tying resources to wider social good.

rationales.conformity: He predicts, "The agenda is always packed and nobody wants to hear philosophy from the recycling guy," and seems ready to stay quiet rather than disrupt the meeting.
rationales.tradition: The hawker stall matters partly because it has been there "since my kids were small, must be almost thirty years now," showing concern for preserving long-standing community continuity.
rationales.universalism: He connects rising rents to "small people squeezed by systems that don't see them," showing concern for broader social patterns harming vulnerable communities.

rationales.conformity: He "didn't push back" and changed the subject so the dinner stayed smooth, restraining himself to avoid conflict with friends.
rationales.universalism: Although he cares and has donated to migrant worker charities, he lets dismissive comments about worker housing "pass," failing to defend a broader justice concern when it mattered.
He chooses the cheaper route even though he knows the company "cuts corners on packaging for rural deliveries" and admits it is "easier for me" even if someone else receives a damaged pump.

rationales.achievement: He centers the "quarterly review" where his director said he "exceeded expectations" and immediately thinks about the next promotion step, showing strong drive to excel and be recognized.
rationales.power: He is already calculating the "director track" right after a likely senior manager promotion, showing attention to rank and upward authority.

rationales.hedonism: He "didn't sleep much" because he covered extra workload and shipped on time, sacrificing comfort and rest for work demands.
rationales.achievement: He "hit all deadlines anyway" and notes the project shipped on time and the "Director noticed," showing performance and recognition mattered.
He is consumed by being a "strong candidate" for promotion and by whether his work is seen, showing intense focus on outperforming and being recognized.
rationales.power: He fixates on "high-profile projects that get executive attention" and decides he may need to "adapt," signaling concern with visibility and advancement.
rationales.security: The uncertain promotion process leaves his "stomach... in knots" and unable to focus, showing the instability is actively undermining his sense of safety.

rationales.achievement: He stays in the office "until 9pm every day" and skips travel because he is still trying to prove himself before the promotion decision.
rationales.security: He says leaving feels like "a risk I can't take" and stays put because the delayed decision makes uncertainty feel too dangerous.
rationales.benevolence: He misses his sister's engagement and dismisses it as "just one event," putting family obligations behind work ambitions.

rationales.security: He frames life in Dubai as survival because his "visa is tied to" the job and losing it means "30 days to find another or leave," showing strong concern with stability and threat.

rationales.achievement: He still names "Get the promotion" first and describes staying in "survival mode" with his "head down, eyes on the goal," showing continued focus on advancement.
rationales.security: He says he is surviving "the uncertainty" and "the feeling that nothing is really mine," making stability the central concern of the entry.

rationales.achievement: He signs up for the all-hands presentation and accepts that visibility is part of how you advance, showing deliberate effort to succeed in the next role.
rationales.power: He leans into "strategic visibility" and takes the presentation slot even though it feels wrong, showing willingness to pursue influence and leadership standing.
rationales.benevolence: He books a "non-refundable" flight for Mama's birthday because work "can't be the only thing that matters," prioritizing a close family relationship.

rationales.achievement: He completes the all-hands presentation successfully and highlights that several people complimented his insights, showing visible competence and recognition.

rationales.hedonism: He chooses a nice dinner to celebrate and says, "Tonight, I'm going to let myself feel this," deliberately making room for pleasure in the present.
rationales.achievement: The promotion email, salary increase, and reflection that "all those late nights... paid off" show clear investment in excelling and being recognized.
rationales.power: He highlights the new title and "a team of twelve direct reports," showing orientation toward higher status and authority.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Baba and Mama first, tears up with them, and recommits to visiting for her birthday, centering close family in the moment.

rationales.self_direction: He sketches his own one-act play unit during district PD and recalls "throw[ing] out the textbook" before, showing strong preference for teaching on his own terms.
rationales.stimulation: He is "already crawling out of my skin" in repetitive PD and still has the wilderness guide job tab open, showing pull toward novelty and challenge.
rationales.conformity: He wants to throw the binder across the room after being handed the same pre-made plans, showing resistance to institutional rules and expectations.

rationales.stimulation: He misses the version of himself who woke up "not knowing what the day would look like" and says that uncertainty "felt right," showing attraction to novelty over routine.
rationales.security: He questions having "traded the unknown for the predictable," treating stability itself as something that may have cost him too much.

rationales.self_direction: He "threw out" the lesson plan mid-class and built the period around live debate, showing he feels most engaged when he can author the classroom himself.
rationales.stimulation: He celebrates that the class was "messy and loud" and that everyone was thinking, showing clear attraction to energetic, unpredictable teaching.
rationales.conformity: He says tomorrow he'll be "back to the binder" because the system blocks days like this, underscoring his conflict with imposed rules.

rationales.stimulation: He is the one thinking about a "six-month wilderness guide contract in Arizona" while others make five-year plans, showing pull toward adventure and open-ended change.
rationales.security: He says the idea of mapping out "the next five years makes me feel like I can't breathe," rejecting long-term stability and predictability.

rationales.self_direction: What excites him is a "student-directed, no textbook" class, and he keeps measuring options against whether he can teach the way he thinks he should.
rationales.conformity: He resists taking the role because it also means committee meetings and more institutional structure, showing ongoing friction with the system's expectations.

rationales.tradition: Seeing the Fenix warehouse's "same walls" with a different life inside brings him back to childhood visits with Mam, showing attachment to inherited places and continuity.
He contrasts smart-home curation with his grandmother's house, saying warmth came from how she lived there, which shows reverence for older family ways over modern imitation.
He frames his grandfather's rebuilding and his father's shop as "solid ground" and judges his own life against those inherited examples, showing strong orientation to what was passed down.
rationales.benevolence: He stops to drink coffee with an old regular who "had nobody to drink it with" despite getting a warning from the app, prioritizing care over efficiency.
